tools.name: SIP Calculator
tools.shortDescription: Calculate your SIP returns with monthly investment, expected rate, and time period.
tools.seoTitle: SIP Calculator - Calculate SIP Returns Online Free
tools.metaDescription: Use our free SIP calculator to estimate your Systematic Investment Plan returns. Enter monthly amount, rate of return, and tenure to see wealth growth.
tools.content:
A Systematic Investment Plan (SIP) calculator is one of the most powerful financial planning tools available to investors today. Whether you are a seasoned investor or just starting your wealth-building journey, understanding how your SIP investments will grow over time is critical for achieving your financial goals.

Our free SIP calculator helps you estimate the future value of your monthly mutual fund investments. By entering three simple values — your monthly investment amount, the expected annual rate of return, and the investment duration — you instantly get a projection of your total invested amount versus total returns.

How Does a SIP Calculator Work?

The SIP calculator uses compound interest formula to compute returns. The mathematical formula used is: M = P × ({[1 + i]^n – 1} / i) × (1 + i), where M is the maturity amount, P is the monthly investment, i is the monthly rate of interest, and n is the number of payments (months).

For example, if you invest ₹5,000 per month at 12% annual return for 10 years, you would invest a total of ₹6,00,000 but your wealth could grow to approximately ₹11.6 lakhs, giving you returns of over ₹5.6 lakhs.

Why Use a SIP Calculator?

SIP calculators provide instant clarity on your investment outcomes without complex manual calculations. They help you plan for specific financial goals such as buying a home, funding children's education, or building a retirement corpus. You can experiment with different scenarios — increasing your monthly SIP amount, adjusting return expectations, or extending the tenure — to see how each variable impacts your wealth.

Benefits of SIP Investing

SIPs offer the advantage of rupee cost averaging, meaning you buy more units when markets are low and fewer when they are high. This disciplined approach reduces the impact of market volatility over time. Starting early is the most important factor in SIP investing because compounding works exponentially over long periods.

Even a modest monthly SIP of ₹1,000 started at age 25 could grow significantly more than ₹5,000 started at age 40, due to the power of time and compound interest.

Tips for Using This SIP Calculator

Always use a conservative expected return rate (8–10% for equity mutual funds over long periods). Consider step-up SIP where you increase your monthly contribution by 10% every year. Use the calculator to set concrete, goal-based targets rather than arbitrary amounts. Regular review and rebalancing of your mutual fund portfolio is essential for staying on track.

faq.question: What is a SIP calculator?
faq.answer: A SIP calculator is an online tool that estimates the future value of your Systematic Investment Plan (SIP) based on monthly investment amount, expected annual return rate, and investment duration using compound interest formula.

faq.question: How accurate is the SIP return calculation?
faq.answer: The SIP calculator provides estimates based on a constant assumed rate of return. Actual mutual fund returns vary with market conditions. The calculator is best used for planning and goal-setting purposes.

faq.question: What is a good expected return rate to use in SIP calculator?
faq.answer: For equity mutual funds, an expected return of 10–12% per annum is commonly used for long-term projections (10+ years). For debt funds, 6–8% is more appropriate. Always use conservative estimates for financial planning.

faq.question: Can I calculate step-up SIP returns?
faq.answer: Yes, you can use our advanced SIP calculator that supports step-up SIP, where your monthly investment increases by a fixed percentage each year, typically 10%, to match income growth.

faq.question: Is there a minimum SIP amount?
faq.answer: Most mutual funds allow SIPs starting from ₹100 or ₹500 per month. However, for meaningful wealth creation, financial advisors recommend investing at least ₹1,000–₹5,000 per month depending on your income and goals.

tools.name: EMI Calculator
tools.shortDescription: Calculate your loan EMI for home, car, or personal loans instantly.
tools.seoTitle: EMI Calculator - Home, Car & Personal Loan EMI
tools.metaDescription: Free EMI calculator for home loan, car loan, and personal loan. Calculate monthly EMI, total interest, and payment schedule instantly.
tools.content:
An EMI (Equated Monthly Installment) calculator is an essential financial tool for anyone planning to take a loan. Whether it is a home loan, car loan, personal loan, or education loan, knowing your monthly EMI before committing helps you plan your budget and make informed borrowing decisions.

Our free EMI calculator provides instant results with three simple inputs: loan principal amount, annual interest rate, and loan tenure in months or years. Within seconds, you see your monthly EMI, total amount payable, and total interest charged over the loan period.

How is EMI Calculated?

EMI is calculated using the standard reducing balance method: EMI = P × r × (1+r)^n / ((1+r)^n – 1), where P is the principal loan amount, r is the monthly interest rate (annual rate / 12), and n is the loan tenure in months.

For example, a home loan of ₹30 lakhs at 8.5% per annum for 20 years results in a monthly EMI of approximately ₹26,035. Over 20 years, you pay a total of ₹62.5 lakhs, meaning the interest component is ₹32.5 lakhs — more than the principal itself.

Home Loan EMI Planning

For home loans, banks typically allow EMI up to 40–50% of your monthly gross income. If your monthly income is ₹80,000, you can comfortably afford an EMI of ₹32,000–₹40,000. Use our calculator to find the right loan amount that keeps your EMI affordable.

Car Loan EMI Tips

Car loans typically have tenures of 3–7 years with interest rates ranging from 7.5% to 14%. A shorter tenure means higher EMI but significantly lower total interest. A 5-year car loan at 9% on ₹8 lakhs costs ₹16,598 per month and ₹1.99 lakh total interest.

Personal Loan Considerations

Personal loans have no collateral and typically carry higher interest rates (12–24%). Always compare the total cost of borrowing, not just the EMI. Prepayment can save substantial interest amounts.

Amortization Schedule

Our EMI calculator also shows an amortization breakdown, revealing how much of each payment goes toward principal versus interest. In the early months of a loan, most of your EMI pays interest. Over time, the principal component increases — this is especially important to understand for home loans where prepayment in early years saves the most interest.

faq.question: What does EMI stand for?
faq.answer: EMI stands for Equated Monthly Installment. It is the fixed monthly payment made to a lender (bank or NBFC) to repay a loan. Each EMI includes both the principal repayment and interest for that month.

faq.question: Does EMI change if interest rate changes?
faq.answer: For floating rate loans, if the bank's benchmark rate (like MCLR or repo rate) changes, your EMI may increase or decrease. Fixed rate loans maintain the same EMI throughout the tenure.

faq.question: What happens if I miss an EMI payment?
faq.answer: Missing an EMI results in a late payment penalty (usually 1–3% of overdue amount) and negatively impacts your CIBIL credit score. Consistently missing payments can lead to loan default and legal action.

faq.question: Can I prepay my loan to reduce EMI?
faq.answer: Yes. Most banks allow part-prepayment after a lock-in period. Prepayment reduces your outstanding principal, which can either reduce your monthly EMI or shorten the loan tenure — both save interest.

faq.question: How do I reduce my loan EMI?
faq.answer: You can reduce EMI by negotiating a lower interest rate, choosing a longer tenure, making a larger down payment, or opting for a balance transfer to a lender offering lower rates.

tools.h1: Age Calculator — Calculate Your Exact Age
tools.content:
An age calculator computes your exact age from your date of birth to today or any target date. Beyond simple years, our calculator shows months, weeks, days, hours, and even minutes of life — giving you a fascinating perspective on your time on Earth.

How Age is Calculated

Age calculation must account for leap years, varying month lengths, and timezone differences. Our calculator uses precise date arithmetic to give you accurate results regardless of when your birthday falls.

Uses of an Age Calculator

Legal and administrative: Many legal thresholds depend on exact age — voting rights at 18, retirement benefits, senior citizen discounts. Medical: Doctors calculate patient age precisely for medication dosing, developmental milestones, and age-specific health screenings. Insurance: Age determines premium calculations for life, health, and vehicle insurance policies. Fun facts: See exactly how many days, hours, or heartbeats you've lived.

Birthday Countdown

Our calculator also shows how many days remain until your next birthday — useful for planning celebrations well in advance.

faq.question: How is age calculated exactly?
faq.answer: Age is calculated by subtracting your birth date from today's date, accounting for leap years and varying month lengths. The result gives years, months, and days as separate components.

faq.question: What if I was born on February 29?
faq.answer: For leap year birthdays (Feb 29), most systems count the birthday on Feb 28 in non-leap years. Our calculator handles this correctly and shows your age accurately.

faq.question: Can I calculate age between two specific dates?
faq.answer: Yes. Our calculator supports custom date ranges — enter any start and end date to calculate the age or duration between them, not just from birth to today.

tools.h1: BMI Calculator — Body Mass Index Calculator
tools.content:
BMI (Body Mass Index) is a widely used screening tool that estimates body fat based on height and weight. Our free BMI calculator instantly computes your BMI and tells you which category you fall into — underweight, normal weight, overweight, or obese — based on WHO standards.

BMI Formula

BMI = weight (kg) ÷ height² (m²). In imperial: BMI = 703 × weight (lbs) ÷ height² (inches²). Our calculator supports both metric and imperial inputs.

BMI Categories (WHO)

Underweight: BMI below 18.5. Normal weight: BMI 18.5–24.9. Overweight: BMI 25–29.9. Obese Class I: BMI 30–34.9. Obese Class II: BMI 35–39.9. Obese Class III: BMI 40 and above.

Limitations of BMI

BMI does not distinguish between muscle and fat mass. Athletes with high muscle mass may show as overweight despite being very fit. BMI also doesn't account for fat distribution — abdominal fat carries higher health risk than fat elsewhere. Use BMI as one indicator among many, not as a definitive health measure.

faq.question: What is a healthy BMI?
faq.answer: A BMI between 18.5 and 24.9 is considered healthy by WHO standards. However, for Asian populations including India, a BMI above 23 may indicate increased health risk.

faq.question: Is BMI accurate for everyone?
faq.answer: BMI is a population-level screening tool, not a perfect individual measure. It may overestimate fat in muscular people and underestimate it in older adults with low muscle mass. Consult a doctor for personalized assessment.

faq.question: What is the ideal BMI for Indians?
faq.answer: The Indian Council of Medical Research suggests that for Indians, a BMI of 18.0–22.9 is normal, 23–24.9 is overweight, and 25+ is obese — lower thresholds than WHO global standards due to higher metabolic risk at lower BMIs.

tools.name: Calorie Calculator
tools.shortDescription: Calculate daily calorie needs based on age, weight, height, and activity level.
tools.seoTitle: Calorie Calculator - Daily Calorie Needs Calculator Free
tools.metaDescription: Calculate your daily calorie needs for weight loss, maintenance, or muscle gain. Based on age, weight, height, gender and activity level. Free.
tools.content:
Knowing your daily calorie needs is the foundation of any effective diet or fitness plan. Our free calorie calculator uses the Mifflin-St Jeor equation — the most accurate formula for estimating Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) — and multiplies by your activity factor to give your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE).

How Calories are Calculated

BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate): Calories burned at complete rest to maintain vital functions. TDEE = BMR × Activity Factor. Activity factors range from 1.2 (sedentary) to 1.9 (very active). To lose weight: eat 500 calories below TDEE. To gain weight: eat 300–500 calories above TDEE.

Mifflin-St Jeor Formula

Men: BMR = (10 × weight kg) + (6.25 × height cm) − (5 × age) + 5. Women: BMR = (10 × weight kg) + (6.25 × height cm) − (5 × age) − 161.

faq.question: How many calories should I eat to lose weight?
faq.answer: A deficit of 500 calories per day leads to approximately 0.5 kg weight loss per week. Never go below 1200 calories (women) or 1500 calories (men) without medical supervision.

faq.question: What is TDEE?
faq.answer: TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure) is the total calories you burn in a day including exercise and daily activities. Eating at your TDEE maintains your current weight.

faq.question: Are these calorie estimates accurate?
faq.answer: The Mifflin-St Jeor equation is accurate to within 10% for most people. Individual metabolism varies — use these as starting estimates and adjust based on actual results over 2–3 weeks.

tools.h1: Compound Interest Calculator — Grow Your Wealth
tools.content:
Compound interest is the eighth wonder of the world — money that earns interest on its interest, creating exponential growth over time. Our compound interest calculator shows you exactly how your principal grows with different compounding frequencies and rates.

Compound Interest Formula

A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), where A = Final amount, P = Principal, r = Annual rate (decimal), n = Compounding periods per year, t = Time in years.

Compounding Frequencies

Annually (n=1): Interest added once per year. Quarterly (n=4): Interest added 4 times per year. Monthly (n=12): Most common for savings accounts and loans. Daily (n=365): Maximum compounding, used by some high-yield accounts.

Power of Compound Interest

₹1,00,000 at 10% for 20 years: Simple interest = ₹3,00,000. Compound interest (annual) = ₹6,72,750. Compound interest (monthly) = ₹7,32,857. The difference is the compounding effect — and it grows exponentially with time.

faq.question: What is the difference between simple and compound interest?
faq.answer: Simple interest is calculated only on the principal. Compound interest is calculated on principal plus accumulated interest — meaning interest earns interest, creating exponential growth over time.

faq.question: Which compounding frequency is best?
faq.answer: More frequent compounding gives slightly higher returns. Daily compounding yields marginally more than monthly, which yields more than annual. For long time horizons, the difference compounds significantly.

faq.question: How do I use compound interest for investments?
faq.answer: Start early and stay invested. ₹10,000 compounding at 12% for 30 years grows to ₹2,99,599. The same amount for 20 years grows to only ₹96,463 — time is the most powerful variable in compound interest.

tools.h1: Discount Calculator — Calculate Sale Price & Savings
tools.content:
Whether you're shopping online, calculating trade discounts, or figuring out sale prices, our discount calculator gives you instant answers. Enter any two values — original price and discount percentage, or discounted price and percentage — and get the complete picture.

Discount Formulas

Discounted price = Original price × (1 − discount%/100). Amount saved = Original price × discount%/100. Discount% = (Amount saved ÷ Original price) × 100. Original price = Discounted price ÷ (1 − discount%/100).

Double Discount Calculation

When multiple discounts apply (e.g., 20% off + additional 10% off), they don't simply add up. A 20% discount followed by 10% equals 28% total — not 30%. Our calculator handles successive discounts correctly.

GST After Discount

In India, GST is applied on the post-discount price, not the original price. Our calculator optionally adds GST calculation so you see the exact amount payable after both discount and tax.

faq.question: How do I calculate 20% off a price?
faq.answer: Multiply the original price by 0.20 to get the discount amount, then subtract from original price. Example: 20% off ₹500 = ₹500 × 0.20 = ₹100 discount → Final price = ₹400.

faq.question: How do I find the original price after discount?
faq.answer: Divide the discounted price by (1 − discount percentage/100). Example: If ₹400 is after 20% off: ₹400 ÷ 0.80 = ₹500 original price.

faq.question: Is a 50% discount the same as buy one get one free?
faq.answer: Yes, effectively. Buy one get one free means you pay for one item and get two, which is equivalent to 50% off per item. However, BOGO requires buying two items while a 50% discount applies to any quantity.

tools.h1: GST Calculator — Calculate GST Amount India
tools.content:
GST (Goods and Services Tax) replaced India's complex indirect tax system in 2017, unifying multiple taxes into a single structure. Our free GST calculator handles both GST-exclusive (add GST to base price) and GST-inclusive (extract GST from total price) calculations for all GST slabs.

GST Slabs in India

0%: Essential items — fresh food, books, newspapers, children's drawing books. 5%: Basic necessities — packaged food, footwear under ₹1000, transport services. 12%: Standard items — processed food, business class air travel, mobile phones. 18%: Most services and goods — restaurants, electronics, software, financial services. 28%: Luxury and sin goods — automobiles, tobacco, aerated drinks, luxury hotels.

CGST and SGST Breakdown

For intra-state transactions, GST splits equally: CGST (Central GST) = 50% of total GST. SGST (State GST) = 50% of total GST. For inter-state: IGST (Integrated GST) = Full GST rate applied by central government.

GST Inclusive vs Exclusive

GST exclusive: You have the base price and want to add GST. Final price = Base × (1 + GST%/100). GST inclusive: You have the total price and want to extract GST. Base price = Total ÷ (1 + GST%/100). GST amount = Total − Base price.

faq.question: What are the GST rates in India?
faq.answer: India has 5 GST slabs: 0%, 5%, 12%, 18%, and 28%. Most everyday goods fall under 5–12%, services under 18%, and luxury items under 28%.

faq.question: How do I calculate GST from an inclusive price?
faq.answer: GST Amount = Total Price − (Total Price ÷ (1 + GST Rate/100)). Example: Total price ₹118 with 18% GST: GST = ₹118 − (₹118 ÷ 1.18) = ₹118 − ₹100 = ₹18 GST.

faq.question: What is the difference between CGST, SGST, and IGST?
faq.answer: CGST and SGST apply on intra-state sales — each gets 50% of the total GST rate. IGST applies on inter-state sales and is collected entirely by the central government before distribution.

tools.seoTitle: Percentage Calculator - Calculate % Online Free
tools.metaDescription: Free percentage calculator. Calculate what % of a number is, percentage change, increase or decrease, and find original value from percentage.
keywords: what percent is X of Y
tools.content:
Percentage calculations appear everywhere — exam scores, discounts, tax rates, interest rates, growth rates, and statistics. Our percentage calculator handles all common percentage problems with clear explanations of each formula.

Types of Percentage Calculations

What is X% of Y? (e.g., What is 15% of 500?) Formula: Result = Y × X/100. What percentage is X of Y? (e.g., 75 is what % of 300?) Formula: % = (X ÷ Y) × 100. Percentage increase: % change = ((New − Old) ÷ Old) × 100. Percentage decrease: Same formula, result is negative. Find original from percentage: Original = Value ÷ (Percentage/100).

Common Applications

Exam scores: 45 out of 60 = 75%. GST calculation: 18% of ₹1000 = ₹180. Salary hike: From ₹50,000 to ₹58,000 = 16% increase. Discount: 25% off ₹800 = ₹600 final price. Profit margin: Profit ÷ Cost × 100.

faq.question: How do I calculate percentage of marks?
faq.answer: Divide total marks obtained by maximum marks, then multiply by 100. Example: 450 out of 600 = (450 ÷ 600) × 100 = 75%.

faq.question: How do I calculate percentage increase?
faq.answer: Percentage increase = ((New Value − Old Value) ÷ Old Value) × 100. Example: Price went from ₹200 to ₹250: ((250 − 200) ÷ 200) × 100 = 25% increase.

faq.question: What is percentage vs percentile?
faq.answer: Percentage is a ratio expressed per hundred (you scored 80%). Percentile is a rank — 80th percentile means you scored higher than 80% of test takers. They measure different things.

tools.h1: Retirement Calculator — Plan Your Retirement Corpus
tools.content:
Retirement planning is one of the most critical financial decisions you'll make. With increasing life expectancy, rising healthcare costs, and inflation eroding purchasing power, building an adequate retirement corpus requires early and consistent planning. Our retirement calculator helps you visualize how much you need and how to get there.

How Much Do You Need to Retire?

The standard rule: Save 25× your annual expenses (the 4% withdrawal rule). If you spend ₹5 lakh per year, you need ₹1.25 crore at retirement. In India, with higher inflation (6–7%), a more conservative target is 30× annual expenses.

Inflation Impact on Retirement

At 6% inflation, ₹50,000/month today becomes ₹1,60,357/month in 20 years. Your retirement corpus must account for this purchasing power erosion. Our calculator applies inflation adjustments to give you realistic targets.

Retirement Savings Vehicles in India

EPF: Employer + employee contribution, 8.15% guaranteed interest. NPS: Market-linked returns, tax benefits under 80CCD. PPF: 7.1% guaranteed, 15-year lock-in, completely tax-free. Mutual fund SIPs: Potentially 10–14% returns, market risk. All of these should be part of a diversified retirement strategy.

faq.question: How much should I save for retirement in India?
faq.answer: Aim to save 15–20% of your income from age 25. The target corpus depends on your expected retirement expenses and age — typically 25–30× your annual expenses at retirement.

faq.question: What is the 4% retirement rule?
faq.answer: The 4% rule states you can withdraw 4% of your retirement corpus annually without depleting it over 30 years. For India with higher inflation, 3–3.5% withdrawal rate is more conservative and safer.

faq.question: Should I use NPS or mutual funds for retirement?
faq.answer: Both serve different purposes. NPS offers guaranteed allocation, tax benefits, and pension annuity. Mutual fund SIPs offer higher potential returns and full liquidity. Ideally, use NPS for the fixed income component and SIPs for the equity growth component.

tools.h1: Image Compressor — Compress Images Online Free
tools.content:
Image compression is one of the most critical tasks for web developers, bloggers, e-commerce owners, and digital marketers. Large image files slow down websites, increase bounce rates, and hurt SEO rankings. Our free online image compressor lets you reduce image file size dramatically while maintaining excellent visual quality.

Our tool supports JPG, JPEG, PNG, and WebP formats. The entire compression process happens inside your browser using JavaScript Canvas API — your images are never uploaded to any server, ensuring complete privacy.

Why Compress Images?

Google's Core Web Vitals penalize slow-loading pages. Images typically account for 60–70% of a webpage's total byte weight. Compressing images can reduce page load time by 2–5 seconds on mobile connections. Faster pages rank higher on Google, convert better, and provide a superior user experience.

Lossless vs Lossy Compression

Lossless compression reduces file size without any quality degradation — ideal for logos, screenshots, and graphics with text. Lossy compression (JPEG) achieves much higher compression ratios (50–80% reduction) by removing imperceptible image data. Our tool lets you control the compression level, so you decide the balance between quality and file size.

WebP Format Advantage

WebP images are typically 25–35% smaller than equivalent JPEG or PNG files at the same visual quality. Converting your images to WebP is one of the easiest performance wins for any website. Our tool supports WebP output conversion from JPG and PNG inputs.

Best Practices for Image Optimization

Always compress images before uploading to your website or CMS. Use appropriate dimensions — do not upload a 3000px wide image for a 600px wide column. Use WebP with JPEG fallback for maximum browser compatibility. Lazy-load images below the fold to improve initial page load time.

For e-commerce sites with hundreds of product images, bulk compression can save gigabytes of storage and significantly reduce CDN costs. For bloggers, properly optimized images can cut page load time in half, improving both user experience and Google Search rankings.

faq.question: Does image compression reduce quality?
faq.answer: Lossy compression (used for JPEG) slightly reduces quality in exchange for smaller file sizes. At quality levels of 70–85%, the difference is virtually invisible to human eyes. Lossless compression preserves 100% quality.

Resizing images to the correct dimensions is essential for web performance, social media optimization, and professional presentations. An oversized image wastes bandwidth and slows down your page, while an undersized one looks blurry and unprofessional. Our free image resizer gives you precise control over output dimensions.

Our tool supports resizing by exact pixel width and height, by percentage scale, or by fitting within specific dimensions while maintaining aspect ratio. Supported formats include JPG, PNG, and WebP.

Why Image Dimensions Matter

Web browsers resize oversized images using CSS, but the full-size file still downloads. A 4000×3000px camera image displayed at 800×600px wastes 25 times more bandwidth than necessary. Always serve images at their display size.

Social Media Image Size Guide

Different platforms require specific image dimensions for optimal display. Facebook cover photos should be 820×312px. Instagram posts require 1080×1080px for square format. Twitter header images need 1500×500px. LinkedIn banners are 1128×191px. Our resizer makes it easy to create correctly sized images for every platform.

Aspect Ratio and Cropping

When resizing, maintaining aspect ratio prevents image distortion. Our tool automatically calculates the proportional dimension when you input either width or height. If you need a specific aspect ratio (like 16:9 for video thumbnails or 1:1 for social media), you can enable auto-crop mode.

Batch Resizing for Efficiency

For developers and content teams working with multiple images, our batch resize feature processes entire image sets at once. This is particularly valuable for e-commerce catalogs where hundreds of product images need uniform dimensions.

Image resizing for mobile optimization is critical in a world where over 60% of web traffic comes from smartphones. Mobile-optimized images using responsive sizing can dramatically improve Core Web Vitals scores, particularly Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), which Google uses as a ranking signal.

faq.question: Will resizing an image reduce its quality?

tools.h1: Video Compressor — Compress Video Online Free
tools.content:
Video files are among the largest digital assets you will work with. A single minute of 1080p video can be 100–400MB in size. For websites, apps, email attachments, or social media, large video files create significant upload, download, and storage challenges. Our free video compressor solves this problem with browser-based video compression technology.

Using WebCodecs API and FFmpeg.wasm, our tool compresses video directly in your browser without uploading files to a server. Your privacy is protected, and compression can begin immediately without file size limits imposed by server infrastructure.

Video Compression Techniques

Video compression works by eliminating redundant information between frames (temporal compression) and within individual frames (spatial compression). Modern codecs like H.264 (AVC) and H.265 (HEVC) are highly efficient at this. Our tool uses H.264 encoding by default with configurable quality settings using the CRF (Constant Rate Factor) scale.

Target File Size vs Bitrate Control

You can compress by setting a target file size (e.g., reduce to under 25MB) or by controlling the output bitrate. Lower bitrate means smaller file but lower quality. For general web use, 2–4 Mbps for 1080p video provides excellent quality. For 720p, 1–2 Mbps is sufficient.

Resolution and Frame Rate Optimization

Sometimes the best compression strategy is reducing resolution or frame rate. A 4K video downscaled to 1080p is typically 4x smaller. Reducing frame rate from 60fps to 30fps halves the temporal data. Our tool supports resolution and frame rate adjustment alongside bitrate control.

Platform-Specific Compression Presets

We provide optimized compression presets for YouTube (recommended bitrates per resolution), WhatsApp (under 16MB), Instagram (under 100MB, 1:1 or 4:5 ratio), email attachments, and general web embedding. These presets take the guesswork out of compression settings.

For content creators, bloggers, and marketing teams producing video content regularly, having a fast, free, privacy-respecting video compressor in the browser is a significant workflow improvement. No more waiting for cloud uploads or worrying about sensitive footage being stored on third-party servers.

tools.h1: Video Converter — Convert Video Formats Online Free
tools.content:
Video format compatibility is a constant challenge in digital workflows. A video recorded on iPhone is in MOV format, but it may not play on older Android devices or Windows Media Player. A WebM file optimized for web won't open in Adobe Premiere. Our free video converter eliminates these compatibility headaches by converting between all major video formats entirely within your browser.

Supported Conversions

Our video converter handles all common format conversions: MOV to MP4, AVI to MP4, WebM to MP4, MKV to MP4, MP4 to AVI, MP4 to WebM, and dozens of other format combinations. MP4 with H.264 codec is the universally compatible output for most use cases.

Understanding Video Codecs vs Containers

Many people confuse video format (container) with codec. A .mp4 file is a container that can hold H.264, H.265, or even AV1 video codec. A .mov file is Apple's QuickTime container. When converting, you choose both the container format and the codec. Our tool handles this complexity automatically, selecting optimal codec settings for each output format.

Which Format Should You Use?

MP4 (H.264): Best for universal compatibility — plays on every device, accepted by every platform. WebM (VP9): Best for web browsers and YouTube — similar quality to H.264 at smaller file sizes. MOV: Best for Apple ecosystem and professional video editing. AVI: Legacy format with wide software support but larger files. MKV: Best for high-quality video storage with multiple audio tracks and subtitles.

Audio Conversion Support

When converting video formats, audio tracks are automatically re-encoded to match the target container. You can optionally extract audio as MP3 or AAC, which is useful for creating podcast episodes or audio-only versions from video content.

For content creators uploading to multiple platforms simultaneously, having the right format for each platform is essential. YouTube prefers MP4, Instagram requires specific aspect ratios and duration limits, and TikTok has its own requirements. Our converter handles all these needs efficiently without server uploads or per-conversion fees.

faq.question: What is the best video format for web use?
faq.answer: MP4 with H.264 encoding is the best choice for web compatibility. For modern browsers, WebM with VP9 offers better compression. Always provide MP4 as a fallback.

faq.question: Can I convert video without losing quality?
faq.answer: Every encoding involves some quality loss (transcode loss). To minimize this, use high quality settings in the converter. Converting to the same codec is more quality-preserving than cross-codec conversion.

faq.question: Is there a file size limit for video conversion?
faq.answer: Browser-based conversion is limited by your device's available RAM. Practically, videos up to 2GB can be converted on most modern computers with 8GB or more RAM.

faq.question: How long does video conversion take?
faq.answer: Conversion speed depends on video length, resolution, and your device's CPU. A 5-minute HD video typically converts in 2–5 minutes on a modern computer.

faq.question: Can I convert multiple videos at once?
faq.answer: Currently, our free tool processes one video at a time. For bulk video conversion, we recommend queuing videos one after another while the first conversion completes.

tools.shortDescription: Convert between meters, feet, inches, centimeters, miles, and kilometers.
tools.seoTitle: Free Length Converter - Meters, Feet, Inches & More
tools.metaDescription: Convert length units online for free. Instantly convert between meters, feet, inches, centimeters, kilometers, miles, yards, and nautical miles.
tools.h1: Length Converter — Meters, Feet, Inches & More
tools.content:
Length conversion is one of the most frequently needed mathematical operations in daily life, construction, engineering, cooking, and international travel. Our free length converter handles all major length units with precision up to 10 decimal places, making it suitable for casual use and professional applications alike.

Supported Length Units

Metric System: Kilometer (km), Meter (m), Decimeter (dm), Centimeter (cm), Millimeter (mm), Micrometer (μm), Nanometer (nm). Imperial/US Customary: Mile (mi), Yard (yd), Foot (ft), Inch (in). Nautical: Nautical mile (nmi). Astronomical: Light year (ly), Astronomical Unit (AU).

Common Length Conversions

1 meter = 3.28084 feet = 39.3701 inches. 1 foot = 30.48 centimeters = 0.3048 meters. 1 inch = 2.54 centimeters = 25.4 millimeters. 1 kilometer = 0.621371 miles = 1,093.61 yards. 1 mile = 1.60934 kilometers = 5,280 feet. 1 nautical mile = 1.852 kilometers = 1.15078 miles.

Practical Use Cases

Construction and real estate: Converting between metric (international) and imperial (US/UK) measurements for architectural drawings, floor plans, and material estimates. Travel and navigation: Understanding distances in unfamiliar units when traveling internationally. GPS coordinates and maps sometimes use different unit systems. Fashion and clothing: Converting sizes between US, UK, and European sizing systems which use different measurement units. Manufacturing: Engineering drawings may specify dimensions in millimeters while manufacturing equipment calibration uses inches. Screen resolutions: Converting between physical screen sizes (inches/cm) and pixel dimensions.

Metric vs Imperial: Why Two Systems?

The metric system (SI) is used by 195 countries worldwide, including all of Europe, Asia, and Africa. The United States, Liberia, and Myanmar are the only countries that primarily use the imperial system. Scientists worldwide use metric exclusively. The 1999 Mars Climate Orbiter crash was caused by a NASA team using imperial units while another used metric — a $327 million lesson in unit conversion.

faq.question: How many centimeters in an inch?
faq.answer: 1 inch = 2.54 centimeters exactly. This is the internationally defined conversion. So 12 inches (1 foot) = 30.48 cm, and 1 meter = 39.3701 inches.

faq.question: How many feet in a meter?
faq.answer: 1 meter = 3.28084 feet (rounded). More precisely, 1 meter = 3 feet and 3.37 inches. For quick mental math, multiply meters by 3.3 to get approximate feet.

faq.question: What is the difference between a mile and a nautical mile?
faq.answer: A statute mile (land mile) = 1.60934 km = 5,280 feet. A nautical mile = 1.852 km = 6,076 feet. The nautical mile is based on the Earth's circumference, making it ideal for navigation where one nautical mile equals one arcminute of latitude.

faq.question: How do I convert meters to feet in my head?
faq.answer: A quick mental approximation: multiply meters by 3.3 (instead of the exact 3.28084). So 10 meters ≈ 33 feet. For running distances, 100 meters ≈ 109 yards.

faq.question: What is a light year in kilometers?
faq.answer: 1 light year = 9.461 × 10^12 km (about 9.46 trillion kilometers). It is the distance light travels in one year at the speed of 299,792 km/s. The nearest star system, Alpha Centauri, is 4.37 light years away.

tools.name: Temperature Converter
tools.shortDescription: Convert between Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin, and Rankine temperature scales.
tools.seoTitle: Free Temperature Converter - Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin
tools.metaDescription: Convert temperature units online for free. Instantly convert between Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin, and Rankine with formulas and reference points.
tools.h1: Temperature Converter — Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin & Rankine
tools.content:
Temperature conversion is one of the most commonly needed calculations, especially when cooking, traveling, or reading international weather forecasts. Unlike most unit conversions, temperature scales use different zero points and different scales, requiring formulas rather than simple multiplication.

Temperature Scales Explained

Celsius (°C): Used by most of the world. Water freezes at 0°C and boils at 100°C at standard pressure. Fahrenheit (°F): Used primarily in the United States. Water freezes at 32°F and boils at 212°F. Kelvin (K): The SI unit of thermodynamic temperature used in science. Absolute zero (0 K) is the coldest possible temperature, −273.15°C. Rankine (°R): An absolute temperature scale using Fahrenheit-sized degrees, used in some engineering applications in the US.

Conversion Formulas

°F = (°C × 9/5) + 32. °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9. K = °C + 273.15. °C = K − 273.15. °R = (°C + 273.15) × 9/5.

Key Reference Points

Absolute zero: −273.15°C = −459.67°F = 0 K. Water freezes: 0°C = 32°F = 273.15 K. Room temperature: ~20°C = 68°F = 293.15 K. Body temperature: 37°C = 98.6°F = 310.15 K. Water boils: 100°C = 212°F = 373.15 K.

faq.question: How do I convert Celsius to Fahrenheit?
faq.answer: Multiply by 9/5 then add 32. Formula: °F = (°C × 1.8) + 32. Example: 25°C = (25 × 1.8) + 32 = 77°F. Quick estimate: double the Celsius and add 30.

faq.question: What is absolute zero in Celsius and Fahrenheit?
faq.answer: Absolute zero is 0 Kelvin = −273.15°C = −459.67°F. It is the theoretical lowest possible temperature, where all atomic motion ceases. It has never been fully achieved in practice.

faq.question: What temperature is the same in Celsius and Fahrenheit?
faq.answer: −40 degrees is the point where Celsius and Fahrenheit are equal: −40°C = −40°F. This is the only temperature where the two scales intersect.

faq.question: What is normal human body temperature in Celsius?
faq.answer: Average human body temperature is approximately 37°C (98.6°F). Normal body temperature varies between individuals, typically ranging from 36.1°C (97°F) to 37.2°C (99°F).

faq.question: What is the difference between Kelvin and Celsius?
faq.answer: Kelvin and Celsius use the same size degree, but differ only in their zero point. Kelvin starts at absolute zero (−273.15°C), while Celsius starts at the freezing point of water. To convert: K = °C + 273.15.

tools.h1: Area Converter — m², Acres, Hectares, sq ft & More
tools.content:
Area conversion is commonly needed in real estate, agriculture, construction, mapping, and land management. Our free area converter supports all standard metric and imperial area units, helping you compare land sizes, room dimensions, and geographic regions with ease.

Supported Area Units

Metric System: Square Kilometer (km²), Hectare (ha), Are (a), Square Meter (m²), Square Decimeter (dm²), Square Centimeter (cm²), Square Millimeter (mm²). Imperial/US Customary: Square Mile (mi²), Acre, Square Yard (yd²), Square Foot (ft²), Square Inch (in²). Nautical: Square Nautical Mile.

Common Area Conversions

1 hectare = 10,000 m² = 2.47105 acres. 1 acre = 4,046.86 m² = 0.404686 hectares. 1 square mile = 640 acres = 258.999 hectares. 1 square meter = 10.7639 square feet. 1 square foot = 0.0929 square meters.

Practical Use Cases

Real estate: Property listings in different countries use different units — m² in Europe and Asia, square feet in the US and UK, and acres or hectares for land. Agriculture: Farm plots, crop fields, and irrigation coverage are measured in acres (US) or hectares (international). Urban planning: City areas, parks, and zoning maps use km² or square miles depending on the country. Construction: Floor plans and room sizes are typically measured in m² or sq ft.

faq.question: How many square feet in a square meter?
faq.answer: 1 square meter = 10.7639 square feet. To quickly estimate, multiply square meters by 10.76. For example, a 50 m² apartment = approximately 538 square feet.

faq.question: How many acres in a hectare?
faq.answer: 1 hectare = 2.47105 acres. A hectare is 10,000 square meters (100m × 100m). An acre is 4,046.86 m². Hectares are used internationally while acres are common in the US and UK.

faq.question: How big is an acre?
faq.answer: 1 acre = 4,046.86 square meters = 43,560 square feet. Visually, an acre is approximately the size of a standard American football field (excluding end zones). It is 1/640th of a square mile.

faq.question: How many square meters in a square kilometer?
faq.answer: 1 square kilometer = 1,000,000 square meters (1 km × 1 km). It is also equal to 100 hectares or 247.105 acres.

faq.question: What is the difference between a hectare and an acre?
faq.answer: A hectare (metric) = 10,000 m² = 2.471 acres. An acre (imperial) = 4,046.86 m². Hectares are the standard unit in most of the world for land measurement, while acres are used primarily in the US, UK, and a few other countries.

tools.h1: Volume Converter — Liters, Gallons, Cups, ml & More
tools.content:
Volume conversion is essential in cooking, chemistry, fuel measurement, and international trade. Our free volume converter supports metric SI units as well as both US customary and UK imperial volume units, since a US gallon and a UK gallon are different sizes.

Supported Volume Units

Metric: Cubic Meter (m³), Hectoliter (hL), Liter (L), Deciliter (dL), Centiliter (cL), Milliliter (mL), Cubic Centimeter (cm³). US Customary: US Gallon, US Quart, US Pint, US Cup, US Fluid Ounce, US Tablespoon, US Teaspoon. UK Imperial: UK Gallon, UK Quart, UK Pint, UK Fluid Ounce.

US vs UK Volume Units

A US gallon = 3.78541 liters. A UK (imperial) gallon = 4.54609 liters — about 20% larger. A US pint = 473 mL while a UK pint = 568 mL. This difference is important for fuel economy, brewing, and recipe conversion between US and British cookbooks.

Common Volume Conversions

1 liter = 0.264172 US gallons = 33.814 US fluid ounces. 1 US gallon = 3.78541 liters = 4 US quarts = 8 US pints. 1 US cup = 236.588 mL = 8 US fluid ounces. 1 US tablespoon = 14.7868 mL = 3 teaspoons.

faq.question: How many ml in a cup?
faq.answer: 1 US cup = 236.588 mL (approximately 237 mL). A metric cup (used in Australia, Canada) = 250 mL. A UK cup is not a standard legal measure but is commonly taken as 284 mL.

faq.question: How many liters in a gallon?
faq.answer: 1 US gallon = 3.78541 liters. 1 UK (imperial) gallon = 4.54609 liters. Be careful to distinguish between US and UK gallons, as they differ by about 20%.

faq.question: What is the difference between a US fluid ounce and a UK fluid ounce?
faq.answer: 1 US fluid ounce = 29.5735 mL. 1 UK fluid ounce = 28.4131 mL. They are close but not identical. In recipes, it is usually safe to treat them as equivalent unless extreme precision is required.

faq.question: How many tablespoons in a cup?
faq.answer: 1 US cup = 16 tablespoons = 48 teaspoons = 8 fluid ounces = 236.588 mL. A half cup = 8 tablespoons, and a quarter cup = 4 tablespoons.

faq.question: How do I convert mL to fluid ounces?
faq.answer: Divide milliliters by 29.5735 to get US fluid ounces. For a quick estimate, divide mL by 30. Example: 500 mL ÷ 29.57 ≈ 16.9 fl oz (a standard water bottle).

tools.h1: Speed Converter — km/h, mph, m/s, Knots & More
keywords: miles per hour to kilometers per hour
tools.content:
Speed conversion is used in automotive, aviation, maritime, and scientific contexts. Our free speed converter covers everything from everyday driving speeds to the speed of light, supporting professional and educational use.

Supported Speed Units

Common: Meter per second (m/s), Kilometer per hour (km/h), Mile per hour (mph), Foot per second (ft/s), Knot (kn). Scientific: Speed of Light (c), Mach (speed of sound at sea level), Centimeter per second (cm/s). Other: Mile per minute, Kilometer per minute, Foot per minute.

Common Speed Conversions

1 km/h = 0.621371 mph = 0.277778 m/s. 1 mph = 1.60934 km/h = 0.44704 m/s. 1 knot = 1.852 km/h = 1.15078 mph. 1 Mach ≈ 340.29 m/s = 1,225 km/h at sea level. Speed of light = 299,792,458 m/s = 1,079,252,848 km/h.

Speed in Context

Walking speed: ~5 km/h (3.1 mph). Cycling: ~25 km/h (15.5 mph). Highway driving: ~120 km/h (74.6 mph). Commercial jet: ~900 km/h (559 mph, ~0.85 Mach). Space shuttle orbital: ~28,000 km/h (17,398 mph).

faq.question: How do I convert km/h to mph?
faq.answer: Multiply km/h by 0.621371 to get mph. Quick estimate: multiply by 0.6. For example, 100 km/h ≈ 62 mph. To convert mph to km/h, multiply by 1.60934.

faq.question: What is a knot in km/h?
faq.answer: 1 knot = 1.852 km/h = 1.15078 mph. Knots (nautical miles per hour) are used in aviation and maritime navigation. A ship traveling at 20 knots is moving at 37 km/h.

faq.question: What is Mach 1 in km/h?
faq.answer: Mach 1 is the speed of sound, which at sea level (15°C) is approximately 340.29 m/s = 1,225 km/h = 761.2 mph. The speed of sound varies with temperature and altitude.

faq.question: How fast is the speed of light in mph?
faq.answer: The speed of light in a vacuum is exactly 299,792,458 m/s = 1,079,252,848 km/h = 670,616,629 mph. Light can travel around the Earth about 7.5 times per second.

faq.question: What is the difference between speed and velocity?
faq.answer: Speed is a scalar quantity (magnitude only), while velocity is a vector (magnitude + direction). A car driving at 60 mph in a circle has constant speed but changing velocity.

tools.content:
Time conversion is used across computing, physics, project management, and everyday life. Our free time converter supports everything from picoseconds (used in computing) to centuries (used in history), making it useful for scientists, programmers, students, and project planners.

Supported Time Units

Common: Week, Day, Hour, Minute, Second. Small (Scientific): Millisecond (ms), Microsecond (μs), Nanosecond (ns), Picosecond (ps). Large (Historical): Month (average), Year, Decade, Century.

Common Time Conversions

1 minute = 60 seconds. 1 hour = 3,600 seconds. 1 day = 86,400 seconds. 1 week = 604,800 seconds. 1 year = 31,557,600 seconds. 1 millisecond = 0.001 seconds.

Fun Time Facts

A nanosecond is 10^-9 seconds — in one nanosecond, light travels about 30 cm (1 foot). Your computer performs billions of operations per second. The human blink lasts approximately 150–400 milliseconds. Light from the Sun takes 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth.

faq.question: How many seconds in a day?
faq.answer: 1 day = 24 hours × 60 minutes × 60 seconds = 86,400 seconds. A year has about 31,536,000 seconds (365 days), or 31,557,600 seconds including the average leap year fraction.

faq.question: How many hours in a year?
faq.answer: 1 year = 365 days × 24 hours = 8,760 hours. Including the leap year fraction (365.25 days), 1 year ≈ 8,766 hours. A decade = 87,660 hours.

faq.question: What is a millisecond?
faq.answer: 1 millisecond (ms) = 0.001 seconds = 1/1,000 of a second. Human reaction time is approximately 150–300 ms. Internet ping times are measured in milliseconds.

faq.question: How many days in a year?
faq.answer: A common year has 365 days. A leap year has 366 days, occurring every 4 years (with exceptions for century years). The average year length is 365.2425 days.

faq.question: What is the difference between a microsecond and a nanosecond?
faq.answer: 1 microsecond (μs) = 0.000001 seconds = 1,000 nanoseconds. 1 nanosecond (ns) = 0.000000001 seconds. Modern CPUs operate at GHz speeds, executing instructions every few nanoseconds.

tools.h1: Pressure Converter — Pa, Bar, PSI, atm & More
tools.content:
Pressure conversion is critical in engineering, meteorology, medicine, and industrial applications. Our free pressure converter supports all major pressure units, from the SI standard Pascal to the commonly used PSI in automotive and industrial settings.

Supported Pressure Units

SI/Metric: Pascal (Pa), Hectopascal (hPa), Kilopascal (kPa), Megapascal (MPa), Bar, Millibar (mbar). Imperial: PSI (pounds per square inch), PSF (pounds per square foot), Inch of Mercury (inHg). Atmosphere-Based: Atmosphere (atm), Torr, Millimeter of Mercury (mmHg), Technical Atmosphere (at).

Common Pressure Conversions

1 bar = 100,000 Pa = 14.5038 PSI. 1 atm = 101,325 Pa = 1.01325 bar = 14.696 PSI. 1 PSI = 6,894.76 Pa = 0.0689476 bar. Standard atmospheric pressure = 1 atm = 1013.25 hPa = 760 mmHg.

Real-World Applications

Tire pressure: Typically measured in PSI (US) or bar (Europe). A standard car tire is inflated to 30–35 PSI (2.1–2.4 bar). Weather: Atmospheric pressure measured in hPa or millibars. Standard sea-level pressure = 1013.25 hPa. Blood pressure: Measured in mmHg. Normal BP is approximately 120/80 mmHg. Scuba diving: Every 10 meters of water depth adds approximately 1 atm of pressure.

faq.question: How do I convert bar to PSI?
faq.answer: Multiply bar by 14.5038 to get PSI. So 2 bar = 29.0 PSI. To convert PSI to bar, divide by 14.5038 or multiply by 0.0689476.

faq.question: What is standard atmospheric pressure in different units?
faq.answer: 1 standard atmosphere (atm) = 101,325 Pa = 1013.25 hPa = 1.01325 bar = 14.696 PSI = 760 mmHg = 760 Torr = 29.921 inHg.

faq.question: What is the difference between PSI and bar?
faq.answer: PSI (pounds per square inch) is common in the US and UK. Bar is used in Europe and most of the world. 1 bar ≈ 14.5 PSI. Both measure the same physical quantity (pressure) with different scales.

faq.question: What is a Pascal?
faq.answer: The Pascal (Pa) is the SI unit of pressure, equal to one Newton per square meter (1 N/m²). Standard atmospheric pressure is 101,325 Pa. Kilopascal (kPa = 1,000 Pa) is more practical for most applications.

faq.question: Why is blood pressure measured in mmHg?
faq.answer: Millimeters of mercury (mmHg) dates back to the first blood pressure devices which used mercury columns. Although mercury is no longer used, mmHg persists in medicine by convention. 1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa.

tools.h1: Energy Converter — Joules, kWh, Calories, BTU & More
tools.content:
Energy conversion is fundamental in physics, nutrition, engineering, and utility billing. Our free energy converter supports all major energy units, from the everyday calorie to the electron-volt used in particle physics.

Supported Energy Units

SI/Metric: Joule (J), Kilojoule (kJ), Megajoule (MJ), Gigajoule (GJ), Newton-meter (N·m). Heat/Caloric: Calorie (cal), Kilocalorie (kcal), British Thermal Unit (BTU), Therm (US). Electrical: Watt-hour (Wh), Kilowatt-hour (kWh), Megawatt-hour (MWh), Electron-volt (eV).

Common Energy Conversions

1 kilocalorie (food calorie) = 4,184 J = 4.184 kJ. 1 kWh = 3,600,000 J = 3.6 MJ. 1 BTU = 1,055.06 J = 0.293071 Wh. 1 therm = 100,000 BTU = 105.48 MJ.

Energy in Daily Life

Food energy: Nutrition labels use kilocalories (kcal), often labelled as 'Calories' (capital C). 1 food Calorie = 1 kcal = 4,184 J. Electricity bills: Energy consumption is billed in kilowatt-hours (kWh). 1 kWh = energy used by a 1,000-watt appliance for 1 hour. Heating: Natural gas and heating oil are measured in BTU or therms in the US. Physics: Particle physicists use electron-volts (eV). 1 eV = 1.602 × 10^-19 J.

faq.question: What is the difference between a calorie and a kilocalorie?
faq.answer: A small calorie (cal) = 4.184 J. A kilocalorie (kcal) = 1,000 cal = 4,184 J. Food nutrition labels use kcal but often write 'Calories' (capital C). So '200 Calories' on a food label = 200 kcal = 836,800 J.

faq.question: How do I convert kWh to joules?
faq.answer: 1 kWh = 3,600,000 joules (3.6 MJ). Multiply kWh by 3,600,000 to get joules. Example: 5 kWh = 18,000,000 J = 18 MJ.

faq.question: What is a BTU?
faq.answer: BTU (British Thermal Unit) is the energy required to raise 1 pound of water by 1°F. 1 BTU = 1,055.06 joules = 0.293 Wh. It is commonly used in the US for air conditioner ratings, heating systems, and gas appliances.

faq.question: How many joules in a kilowatt-hour?
What is an electron-volt?
faq.answer: An electron-volt (eV) = 1.602 × 10^-19 joules. It is the energy gained by a single electron when accelerated through 1 volt. Used in atomic physics, nuclear physics, and chemistry to describe bond and photon energies.

tools.h1: Data Storage Converter — GB, MB, KB, Bits & More
tools.content:
Digital data conversion is essential in computing, networking, storage, and software development. Our data converter supports both decimal (SI) units used by hard drive manufacturers and binary (IEC) units used by operating systems, as well as data transfer rate units.

Decimal (SI) vs Binary (IEC) Units

A frequent source of confusion: storage manufacturers use decimal prefixes (1 KB = 1,000 bytes) while operating systems use binary prefixes (1 KiB = 1,024 bytes). This is why a '1 TB' hard drive shows as ~931 GiB in Windows or macOS. Decimal: bit (b), Byte (B), Kilobyte (KB=1,000 B), Megabyte (MB=10^6 B), Gigabyte (GB=10^9 B), Terabyte (TB=10^12 B), Petabyte (PB=10^15 B). Binary (IEC): Kibibyte (KiB=1,024 B), Mebibyte (MiB=1,024 KiB), Gibibyte (GiB=1,024 MiB), Tebibyte (TiB=1,024 GiB).

Data Transfer Rates

Network speeds are measured in bits per second (not bytes). 1 Mbps = 1,000,000 bits/s ≈ 125 KB/s (divide by 8 to get bytes). A 100 Mbps internet connection transfers about 12.5 MB per second.

faq.question: How many MB in a GB?
faq.answer: 1 GB (gigabyte, decimal) = 1,000 MB. 1 GiB (gibibyte, binary) = 1,024 MiB. Hard drives use GB (decimal), while operating systems typically display GiB but label it 'GB'. This is why a 1 TB drive shows as ~931 GB in Windows.

faq.question: What is the difference between KB and KiB?
faq.answer: KB (kilobyte, SI) = 1,000 bytes. KiB (kibibyte, IEC) = 1,024 bytes. The difference compounds at higher scales: 1 TB = 1,000 GB while 1 TiB = 1,099.5 GB.

faq.question: Why does my hard drive show less space than advertised?
faq.answer: Drive manufacturers use decimal (1 TB = 10^12 bytes) while operating systems report in binary (1 TiB = 2^40 bytes). 1 TB (decimal) = 0.909 TiB ≈ 931 GiB. So a 1 TB drive appears as ~931 GB in your OS.

faq.question: How many bits in a byte?
faq.answer: 1 byte = 8 bits. Internet speeds (Mbps) are in bits, while file sizes (MB) are in bytes — so divide Mbps by 8 to get MB/s download speed.

faq.question: What does Mbps mean?
faq.answer: Mbps = megabits per second = 1,000,000 bits per second. To estimate download speed in MB/s, divide Mbps by 8. A 100 Mbps connection = ~12.5 MB/s.

tools.h1: Fuel Efficiency Converter — MPG, km/L, L/100km & More
tools.content:
Fuel efficiency conversion is needed when comparing vehicles from different countries, evaluating fuel costs, or understanding international car specifications. Our free fuel converter supports all major fuel economy units used worldwide.

Supported Fuel Efficiency Units

MPG (US): Miles per US gallon — the standard in the United States. MPG (UK): Miles per imperial gallon — used in the UK. Since a UK gallon is larger, UK MPG values are about 20% higher for the same efficiency. km/L: Kilometers per liter — common in many countries including India, Japan, and Latin America. L/100km: Liters per 100 kilometers — the EU standard, used in Europe, Australia, and Canada. Miles/Liter and km/gal (US) round out the available units.

Important Note: Inverse Relationship

Fuel consumption (L/100km) is inversely proportional to fuel economy (km/L and MPG). A lower L/100km means better efficiency. A higher MPG or km/L means better efficiency. Formula: L/100km = 235.215 / MPG(US).

Real-World Reference Points

City driving sedan: ~25–30 MPG US / 10.6–12.7 km/L / 7.8–9.4 L/100km. Highway SUV: ~20–25 MPG US / 8.5–10.6 km/L. Hybrid vehicles: ~50+ MPG US / 21+ km/L / under 4.7 L/100km. Large trucks: ~12–18 MPG US / 5.1–7.7 km/L.

faq.question: How do I convert MPG to L/100km?
faq.answer: Use the formula: L/100km = 235.215 ÷ MPG (US). Example: 30 MPG = 235.215 ÷ 30 = 7.84 L/100km. For UK MPG: L/100km = 282.481 ÷ MPG (UK).

faq.question: What is the difference between US MPG and UK MPG?
faq.answer: US MPG uses the US gallon (3.785 liters), while UK MPG uses the imperial gallon (4.546 liters). A car rated at 40 MPG (UK) = only about 33.3 MPG (US). Always check which gallon is used when comparing fuel economy figures.

faq.question: Is lower L/100km better or worse?
faq.answer: Lower L/100km is better — it means the car uses fewer liters of fuel to travel 100 km. A car using 5 L/100km is more efficient than one using 10 L/100km. This is the opposite logic from MPG, where higher is better.

faq.question: How do I convert km/L to MPG?
faq.answer: Multiply km/L by 2.35215 to get US MPG. Example: 15 km/L × 2.35215 = 35.3 MPG (US). For UK MPG, multiply km/L by 2.82481.

faq.question: What fuel efficiency should I aim for?
faq.answer: For a typical sedan, 35+ MPG (US) / 15+ km/L / under 6.7 L/100km is considered good. Hybrids typically achieve 50+ MPG US. Electric vehicles are rated in MPGe (miles per gallon equivalent).

Four tools in one: encode URLs with percent-encoding, decode encoded URLs, parse any URL into its components, and build URLs with a visual query parameter editor.

URL Encoding

Convert special characters to percent-encoded format for safe URL transmission. Characters like spaces become %20, ampersands become %26, etc. Essential for API calls, form submissions, and query parameters.

URL Decoding

Convert percent-encoded URLs back to their original format. Useful for debugging encoded URLs, processing webhook data, and analyzing request logs.

URL Parser

Break any URL into its components: protocol (http/https), hostname, port, pathname, search/query parameters, hash/fragment. Shows each component separately for easy analysis.

URL Builder

When to Use URL Encoding

Link sharing: Ensure URLs work correctly when shared via email or messaging. Cookie values: Cookies often require URL encoding for special characters.

faq.question: What is URL encoding?
faq.answer: URL encoding (percent-encoding) replaces unsafe characters in URLs with a % followed by two hexadecimal digits. For example, a space becomes %20 or +.

faq.question: When should I use %20 vs + for spaces?
faq.answer: Use %20 for path segments and %20 in general. Use + only in application/x-www-form-urlencoded query strings (HTML form submissions).

faq.question: What characters are safe in URLs?
faq.answer: Alphanumeric (a-z, A-Z, 0-9) and special characters: $-_.+!*'(), are safe. All others should be percent-encoded.

faq.question: Can I encode a full URL?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool encodes the entire string. For best results, encode query parameter values separately to preserve the URL structure.

faq.question: What does the URL parser show?
faq.answer: It breaks URLs into protocol, hostname, port, pathname, search params, and hash. Useful for debugging and understanding URL structure.

tools.content:
Convert between Unix timestamps and human-readable dates. Support for both seconds and milliseconds, multiple timezones, ISO 8601 format, and a live real-time Unix clock.

Unix Timestamp Explained

The Unix timestamp (or epoch time) is the number of seconds (or milliseconds) that have elapsed since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 UTC. It's widely used in programming, databases, and APIs for representing dates and times in a system-independent way.

Convert Timestamp to Date

Enter any Unix timestamp (in seconds or milliseconds) to get the corresponding human-readable date. Choose your preferred timezone for the output. See results in multiple formats: local time, UTC, ISO 8601, and RFC 2822.

Convert Date to Timestamp

Select a date and time using the visual date picker. Choose the timezone of your input. Get both seconds and milliseconds timestamps instantly. Perfect for generating timestamps for API calls, database queries, or programming tasks.

Live Current Timestamp

A real-time display shows the current Unix timestamp, updating every second. Shows both seconds and milliseconds versions. Click to copy to clipboard.

What is the Y2K38 Problem?

32-bit signed integer timestamps will overflow on January 19, 2038 at 03:14:07 UTC. Modern 64-bit systems (used by most current software) don't have this limitation and can handle timestamps far into the future.

faq.question: What is a Unix timestamp?
faq.answer: A Unix timestamp is the number of seconds (or milliseconds) that have elapsed since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 UTC (the Unix Epoch). It's widely used in programming.

faq.question: What is the Y2K38 problem?

faq.question: What is the difference between seconds and milliseconds timestamps?
faq.answer: Seconds timestamps have 10 digits (e.g., 1700000000). Milliseconds timestamps have 13 digits (e.g., 1700000000000). Many APIs use seconds, while JavaScript's Date.now() returns milliseconds.

Convert numbers between binary (base 2), octal (base 8), decimal (base 10), hexadecimal (base 16), and any custom base from 2 to 36. Includes bit-level representation and byte count display.

Supported Bases

Binary (base 2): 0-1 — used in computer hardware. Octal (base 8): 0-7 — used in Unix permissions. Decimal (base 10): 0-9 — everyday numbers. Hexadecimal (base 16): 0-9, A-F — used in memory addresses, colors. Custom base (2-36): Any base using 0-9 and A-Z.

Common Use Cases

Programming: Convert between binary, decimal, and hex for debugging and bit manipulation. Unix permissions: Understand chmod values (755 in decimal = 113513 in octal). Color codes: Convert hex colors (#FF5733) to RGB decimal. Data encoding: Work with base64, base32, or other encoding schemes.

Bit-Level Display

Conversion Accuracy

Handles integers up to 2^53-1 (JavaScript's safe integer limit). Supports both positive and negative integers. For large numbers beyond safe integer range, results are displayed with scientific notation.

faq.question: What are the most common number bases in programming?
faq.answer: Binary (base 2) for machine code, octal (base 8) for Unix permissions, decimal (base 10) for everyday use, and hexadecimal (base 16) for memory addresses and color codes.

faq.question: What is hexadecimal used for?
faq.answer: Hexadecimal is compact (1 hex digit = 4 binary bits) and is used for memory addresses, color codes (#FF5733), byte values, and encoding in cryptography.

faq.question: How do I convert binary to decimal?
faq.answer: Multiply each binary digit by 2 raised to its position power, then sum. Example: 1011₂ = 1×8 + 0×4 + 1×2 + 1×1 = 11₁₀.

faq.question: What does octal represent in Unix?
faq.answer: Octal is used for Unix file permissions. 755 means rwxr-xr-x. Each digit represents permissions for owner, group, and others.

faq.question: What is the maximum base supported?
faq.answer: Base 36 (0-9 and A-Z) is supported. This is commonly used for short URLs, license keys, and unique identifiers.

tools.content:
Convert any number from 1 to 3,999,999 to Roman numerals, or decode any Roman numeral back to an Arabic number. Includes a step-by-step breakdown showing how each part is composed.

Roman Numeral Symbols

I = 1, V = 5, X = 10, L = 50, C = 100, D = 500, M = 1000. For numbers above 3999, the tool uses the vinculum (overline) notation: M̅ = 1,000,000, D̅ = 500,000, C̅ = 100,000, etc.

Conversion Rules

Roman numerals are written from largest to smallest value. Subtraction notation is used for 4 (IV), 9 (IX), 40 (XL), 90 (XC), 400 (CD), 900 (CM). The same symbol is never repeated more than three times in a row.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

The converter shows exactly how each number is built. For example: 1999 = M (1000) + CM (900) + XC (90) + IX (9) = MCMXCIX. This helps understand the conversion logic.

Common Uses

Year conversions: 2024 = MMXXIV, 1999 = MCMXCIX, 1776 = MDCCLXXVI. Movie copyright dates, book chapters, clock faces, and Super Bowl numbering. Historical documents and royal titles.

faq.question: What is the largest Roman numeral?
faq.answer: Traditional Roman numerals go up to MMMCMXCIX (3,999). Using vinculum notation for large numbers (like M̅ = 1,000,000), this tool supports up to 3,999,999.

faq.question: How do I write years in Roman numerals?
faq.answer: Years are written using M (1000), C (100), X (10), and I (1). For example: 2024 = MMXXIV, 1999 = MCMXCIX, 1776 = MDCCLXXVI.

faq.question: What is the subtraction rule in Roman numerals?
faq.answer: A smaller numeral before a larger one means subtract. IV = 4 (5-1), IX = 9 (10-1), XL = 40 (50-10), XC = 90 (100-10), CD = 400 (500-100), CM = 900 (1000-100).

faq.question: Can I convert numbers above 3999?
faq.answer: Yes. For numbers above 3,999, the tool uses vinculum notation where an overline multiplies by 1,000. M̅ = 1,000,000, M̅M̅ = 2,000,000, etc.

faq.question: Is there a zero in Roman numerals?
faq.answer: No. The Roman numeral system has no symbol for zero. The concept of zero was introduced later with Arabic numerals.

Translate any text to Morse code or decode Morse back to text. Play Morse code as audio beeps using the Web Audio API. Includes a visual dot-dash display and a complete reference chart for all letters, numbers, and punctuation.

Morse Code Basics

Morse code uses dots (·) and dashes (−) to represent letters and numbers. Dots are short signals, dashes are long signals (three times longer than a dot). The space between parts of the same letter is one dot duration. Space between letters is three dots. Space between words is seven dots.

Audio Playback

Listen to your Morse code with adjustable speed (5-40 WPM). The Web Audio API generates clean sine wave beeps. Perfect for learning Morse code by ear or verifying your encoding.

Visual Display

See your Morse code displayed as dots and dashes with color coding. Letters are grouped visually for easy reading. The display updates in real-time as you type.

Complete Reference Chart

Full reference chart showing all letters (A-Z), numbers (0-9), and common punctuation (., ?, !, @, etc.) with their Morse code equivalents. Great for learning or quick reference.

Common Uses

Ham radio operators, emergency communication, learning Morse code for amateur radio licensing, educational activities, and encoding secret messages.

faq.question: What is Morse code?
faq.answer: Morse code is a communication method using sequences of dots (short signals) and dashes (long signals) to represent letters, numbers, and punctuation. It was used in early telecommunications.

faq.question: How do I separate words in Morse code input?
faq.answer: Separate letters with spaces and words with ' / ' (space-slash-space). For example: 'SOS SOS' = '... --- ... / ... --- ...'

faq.question: Can I listen to the Morse code audio?

faq.question: What is the standard timing for Morse code?
faq.answer: A dot is 1 unit, a dash is 3 units. Space between parts of same letter is 1 unit. Space between letters is 3 units. Space between words is 7 units.

tools.name: Fertilizer (NPK) Calculator
tools.shortDescription: Calculate exact Urea, DAP and MOP quantities from soil test NPK values and ICAR crop requirements.
tools.seoTitle: Free Fertilizer NPK Calculator India — Urea DAP MOP Dose | UtilityMega
tools.metaDescription: Calculate fertilizer (Urea, DAP, MOP) quantities from soil test N-P-K values for any crop. Based on ICAR recommendations. Adjusts for organic matter (FYM, compost, vermicompost).
tools.content:
Enter your soil test N-P-K values and the tool calculates net fertilizer requirement after crediting available soil nutrients. Accounts for organic matter credits from FYM (25 kg N/ha), compost or vermicompost. Outputs Urea, DAP and MOP quantities per acre and total, with approximate cost. Includes a 3-split application schedule (basal + two top-dressings) and a full fertilizer NPK reference table.

How NPK Calculation Works

Crop nutrient requirement (kg/ha) minus soil available nutrients (from soil test) equals net fertilizer requirement. The calculator then converts net requirement to commercial fertilizer quantities based on NPK content of each fertilizer.

Fertilizer NPK Content

Urea: 46% N (Nitrogen). DAP (Diammonium Phosphate): 18% N, 46% P2O5 (Phosphorus). MOP (Muriate of Potash): 60% K2O (Potassium). SSP (Single Super Phosphate): 16% P2O5. NPK Complexes: Various ratios (10-26-26, 12-32-16, etc.).

Soil Test Interpretation

Nitrogen (N): Available N in kg/ha. Typically 200-500 kg/ha in Indian soils. Phosphorus (P): Available P2O5 in kg/ha. Range: 10-100 kg/ha. Potassium (K): Available K2O in kg/ha. Range: 100-400 kg/ha.

Organic Matter Credits

FYM (Farm Yard Manure): 5-10 tons/ha provides 25-50 kg N, 10-20 kg P2O5, 25-50 kg K2O. Compost: Similar to FYM. Vermicompost: 2-5 tons/ha provides 20-40 kg N, 10-20 kg P2O5, 15-30 kg K2O. Green Manure: Provides 40-80 kg N/ha.

Split Application Schedule

Basal dose at sowing: DAP + 1/3 Urea + full MOP. First top-dressing at 30 DAS (days after sowing): 1/3 Urea. Second top-dressing at 60 DAS: 1/3 Urea. This schedule improves nutrient use efficiency.

faq.question: What soil test values are needed?
faq.answer: Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P) and Potassium (K) in kg/hectare. These are obtained from a certified soil testing laboratory (often available free at Krishi Vigyan Kendras).

faq.question: Why subtract soil N from fertilizer dose?
faq.answer: Not all soil nitrogen is available to crops — typically 1–2% of organic N mineralises per season. The calculator credits this so you don't over-apply.

faq.question: What is the split application schedule?
faq.answer: Basal dose at sowing (DAP + 1/3 Urea + full MOP), first top-dressing at 30 DAS (days after sowing), and second top-dressing at 60 DAS.

faq.question: What organic matter credits are available?
faq.answer: FYM (25-50 kg N/ha), Compost, Vermicompost (20-40 kg N/ha), and Green Manure (40-80 kg N/ha).

tools.name: Irrigation Water Calculator
tools.shortDescription: Calculate irrigation water requirements, pump run time and a 7-day irrigation schedule by crop, soil and method.
tools.seoTitle: Free Irrigation Water Calculator India — Pump Hours & Schedule | UtilityMega
tools.metaDescription: Calculate water per irrigation, pump run time and weekly irrigation schedule for any crop. Supports flood, furrow, sprinkler and drip methods. Adjusts for temperature zone and rainfall.
tools.content:
Calculates crop evapotranspiration (ETo × Kc) adjusted for growth stage, temperature zone and effective rainfall. Accounts for irrigation method efficiency (flood 60%, furrow 70%, sprinkler 80%, drip 95%). Outputs litres per irrigation event, pump run time (hours), irrigations per week, total weekly water, and a 7-day schedule. Includes water-saving tips specific to each crop.

Crop Water Requirement Calculation

ETc (crop evapotranspiration) = ETo × Kc. ETo is reference evapotranspiration based on temperature zone. Kc is crop coefficient varying by growth stage (initial, development, mid-season, late).

Temperature Zones

Cool temperate (Himalayan region): ETo 2-3 mm/day. Warm temperate (North India plains): ETo 3-5 mm/day. Semi-arid (Central India): ETo 4-6 mm/day. Arid (Rajasthan, Gujarat): ETo 5-8 mm/day. Humid tropical (Coastal, South India): ETo 3-4 mm/day.

Irrigation Method Efficiency

Flood irrigation: 60% efficiency (40% water loss). Furrow irrigation: 70% efficiency. Sprinkler irrigation: 80% efficiency. Drip irrigation: 95% efficiency — highest water savings.

Effective Rainfall

Rainfall that actually contributes to crop water needs. Typically 70-80% of total rainfall in medium soils, 50-60% in heavy clay, 80-90% in sandy soils.

Pump Run Time Calculation

Water required per irrigation (liters) ÷ Pump discharge (liters per minute) = Pump run time (minutes). Pump discharge depends on pump horsepower and well yield.

7-Day Irrigation Schedule

Outputs a detailed weekly schedule showing which days to irrigate, water quantity per event, and total weekly water. Helps plan irrigation operations efficiently.

faq.question: What is ETo and Kc?
faq.answer: ETo is reference evapotranspiration (water lost from a reference grass crop). Kc is the crop coefficient that adjusts ETo for a specific crop and growth stage. ETo × Kc = actual crop water use (ETc).

faq.question: Why does drip irrigation need less water?
faq.answer: Drip delivers water directly to the root zone with 95% efficiency vs 60% for flood irrigation. Less water is lost to runoff, evaporation and deep percolation.

faq.question: How do I use the pump run time result?
faq.answer: If your pump discharges 1,000 L/min and the result shows 6,000 L per irrigation, you need to run the pump for 6 minutes per irrigation event.

faq.question: What are the different temperature zones in India?
faq.answer: Cool temperate (Himalayan), warm temperate (North plains), semi-arid (Central India), arid (Rajasthan/Gujarat), and humid tropical (coastal/South India).

faq.question: How is effective rainfall calculated?
faq.answer: Effective rainfall is the portion of total rainfall that actually contributes to crop water needs, adjusted for soil type and rainfall intensity.

tools.name: Soil pH Calculator
tools.shortDescription: Check soil pH, see nutrient availability, and calculate lime or sulphur dose to correct soil acidity or alkalinity.
tools.seoTitle: Free Soil pH Calculator — Lime & Sulphur Dose for India | UtilityMega
tools.metaDescription: Enter your soil pH and target, get lime (to raise pH) or sulphur (to lower pH) quantity in kg per acre and total cost. Shows nutrient availability chart and crop pH preferences.
tools.content:
Interactive pH slider (3–10) with colour-coded indicator and category (Extremely Acidic to Strongly Alkaline). Shows nutrient availability (N, P, K, Fe, Zn, Ca) at the current pH. Calculates lime (CaCO₃) dose to raise acidic soil or elemental sulphur dose to lower alkaline soil — per hectare, per acre and total — with approximate cost and application steps. Adjusts buffer factor for sandy, loamy or clay soils.

Soil pH Categories

Extremely Acidic: <4.5 — severe nutrient toxicity. Very Strongly Acidic: 4.5-5.0 — Aluminium toxicity. Strongly Acidic: 5.1-5.5 — Phosphorus fixation. Moderately Acidic: 5.6-6.0 — Optimal for some crops. Slightly Acidic: 6.1-6.5 — Ideal for most crops. Neutral: 6.6-7.3 — Maximum nutrient availability. Slightly Alkaline: 7.4-7.8 — Iron, Zinc deficiency. Moderately Alkaline: 7.9-8.4 — Micronutrient issues. Strongly Alkaline: 8.5-9.0 — Sodium problems. Very Strongly Alkaline: >9.0 — Severe constraints.

Nutrient Availability by pH

At pH 6.0-7.0: All nutrients maximally available. Below pH 5.5: Phosphorus fixed, Aluminium toxic. Above pH 8.0: Iron, Zinc, Manganese, Copper become unavailable. Calcium availability decreases below pH 5.5 and above pH 8.5.

Lime Requirement for Acidic Soil

Agricultural lime (CaCO₃) raises soil pH. Dose depends on current pH, target pH, and soil texture. Sandy soils need less lime, clay soils need more. Apply 2-4 months before planting for best results.

Sulphur Requirement for Alkaline Soil

Elemental sulphur (S) lowers soil pH. Soil bacteria oxidize sulphur to sulphuric acid. Apply 4-6 months before planting. Gypsum (calcium sulphate) can also help but doesn't change pH significantly.

Crop pH Preferences

Rice: 5.5-6.5, Wheat: 6.0-7.5, Maize: 5.8-7.0, Cotton: 6.0-7.5, Sugarcane: 6.0-7.5, Soybean: 6.0-7.0, Groundnut: 5.5-7.0, Potato: 5.0-6.5, Onion: 6.0-7.0, Tomato: 5.5-7.0.

faq.question: What is the ideal soil pH for most crops?
faq.answer: 6.0–7.0 is ideal for most Indian field crops. This range maximises availability of all essential nutrients, especially N, P and K.

faq.question: How long does lime take to work?
faq.answer: Agricultural lime reacts slowly — allow 2–4 weeks after incorporation before sowing. Re-test soil pH after one crop season (3–4 months).

faq.question: Why is pH important for nutrients?
faq.answer: Soil pH controls solubility of nutrients. Phosphorus becomes unavailable below pH 5.5. Iron and zinc become excessive below pH 5.0. Most nutrients are maximally available at pH 6–7.

faq.question: How do I correct alkaline soil?
faq.answer: Apply elemental sulphur (S) which oxidizes to sulphuric acid, lowering pH. Gypsum can help with sodium problems but doesn't significantly change pH.

faq.question: What crops tolerate acidic soil?
faq.answer: Potato (5.0-6.5), Rice (5.5-6.5), Tea (4.5-5.5), Pineapple (4.5-5.5) tolerate acidic conditions well.
