tools.shortDescription: Calculate seed quantity, seed cost and optimum plant population for 8 major crops based on sowing method and germination %.
tools.seoTitle: Free Seed Rate Calculator India — Seed Quantity per Acre | UtilityMega
tools.metaDescription: Calculate how much seed is needed per acre for Rice, Wheat, Maize, Cotton, Soybean and more. Adjusts for sowing method (line, broadcast, transplant) and germination percentage.
tools.content:
Calculates adjusted seed rate for 8 crops based on sowing method (broadcast adds 20%, transplanting uses 5%), actual germination percentage, area and seed price. Shows total seed required in kg, seed cost, and estimated plant population from row × plant spacing. Includes crop-specific tips on seed treatment and germination management. Quick-reference table shows recommended seed rates, 1000-seed weight and spacing for all crops.

Crops Supported

Rice (Paddy), Wheat, Maize (Corn), Cotton, Soybean, Groundnut, Onion, Tomato. Each crop has pre-loaded recommended seed rates and spacing.

Sowing Method Adjustments

Broadcast: Seed scattered manually — requires 20% more seed due to uneven distribution. Line sowing: Drilling in rows — standard seed rate (baseline). Transplanting: Nursery raising then transplanting — uses only 5% of direct-sown seed rate.

Germination Percentage Adjustment

If germination is below 90%, seed rate must be increased. Formula: Adjusted rate = Recommended rate × (90 ÷ Actual germination %). This ensures target plant population.

Spacing and Plant Population

Row spacing × plant spacing determines plant population per unit area. The calculator shows both plant population and recommended spacing for each crop.

Seed Treatment Tips

Rice: Soak in salt solution (remove floating seeds), treat with fungicide. Wheat: Treat with carbendazim or thiram for loose smut. Maize: Treat with thiram or carbendazim. Cotton: Acid delinting required for machine sowing. Soybean: Treat with Rhizobium culture. Groundnut: Treat with thiram or carbendazim. Onion: Treat with thiram for seedling health. Tomato: Treat with trichoderma for disease resistance.

faq.question: Why does germination % affect seed rate?
faq.answer: If germination is 80%, you need to sow 25% more seed to achieve the same plant population as with 100% germination.

faq.question: What is the seed rate for hybrid vs open-pollinated varieties?
faq.answer: Hybrid seeds are typically more expensive and require lower quantities. Single-cross maize hybrids need only 8 kg/acre vs 12 kg for composites.

faq.question: When should I use transplanting?
faq.answer: Transplanting (nursery raising) is used for Rice, Onion and Tomato — it needs only 5% of the direct-sown seed rate but requires nursery infrastructure.

faq.question: What is the recommended seed rate for wheat per acre?
faq.answer: Wheat seed rate is typically 40-50 kg/acre for line sowing, 50-60 kg/acre for broadcast. Adjust based on germination percentage.

faq.question: How is plant population calculated?
faq.answer: Plant population = (Area in m²) ÷ (Row spacing in m × Plant spacing in m). For 1 acre (4,047 m²) with 60 cm × 20 cm spacing: 4,047 ÷ (0.6 × 0.2) = 33,725 plants.

Pesticides Supported

Insecticides: Chlorpyrifos, Lambda-cyhalothrin, Imidacloprid. Fungicides: Mancozeb, Carbendazim, Propiconazole. Herbicides: Glyphosate, 2,4-D Amine, Atrazine.

Spray Volumes per Acre

Drone sprayer: 50 L/acre. High-volume knapsack: 200 L/acre. Power sprayer: 400 L/acre. High-volume nozzles: 100 L/acre. Custom volume input available.

Formulation Types

EC (Emulsifiable Concentrate): Liquid concentrate, mix with water. SC (Suspension Concentrate): Liquid suspension. WP (Wettable Powder): Powder that mixes with water. WG (Water Dispersible Granules): Granules that dissolve.

Per-Tank Mixing Guide

The calculator shows exactly how much pesticide and water to add per tank for your sprayer. This prevents over-application or under-application and ensures uniform coverage.

Safety Guidelines

Always wear PPE: nitrile gloves, safety goggles, respirator mask, full-sleeve clothing, closed-toe shoes. Wash with soap and water immediately after spraying. Never eat, drink, or smoke while spraying. Store pesticides in original containers away from children and food. Follow pre-harvest intervals (PHI) before harvesting treated crops.

Poison Control

In case of pesticide poisoning, call the National Poison Control Center: 1800-11-6117 (Toll-free). Keep pesticide container label handy for ingredients.

faq.question: What is the recommended spray volume per acre?
faq.answer: 200 L/acre for knapsack sprayers, 400 L/acre for power sprayers, 100 L for high-volume nozzles, and 50 L for drone sprayers.

faq.question: How do I convert ml/ha to ml/acre?
faq.answer: Multiply ml/ha × 0.4047 to get ml/acre. For example, 1500 ml/ha = 607 ml/acre.

faq.question: What PPE is required for pesticide spraying?
faq.answer: Minimum: nitrile gloves, safety goggles, a nose-and-mouth respirator mask, full-sleeve clothing and closed-toe shoes. Wash with soap and water immediately after spraying.

faq.question: What is the pre-harvest interval (PHI)?
faq.answer: PHI is the minimum time between pesticide application and crop harvest. Respect PHI to ensure pesticide residues are within safe limits.

faq.question: What should I do in case of pesticide poisoning?

tools.name: Farm Income & Profit Calculator
tools.shortDescription: Track all farm income sources and expenses to calculate net profit, margin and income per acre for a full season P&L.
tools.seoTitle: Free Farm Income Calculator India — Season Profit & Loss | UtilityMega
tools.metaDescription: Add all income sources (crop sale, straw, subsidy) and expenses (seed, fertilizer, labour, irrigation) to get a complete farm P&L with net profit, margin and profit per acre.
tools.content:
A dynamic income and expense tracker for a complete farm P&L statement. Add unlimited income rows (crop sale, by-product sale, government subsidy, agri-tourism) and expense rows (seeds, fertilizer, pesticide, labour, irrigation, harvesting, transport). Outputs total income, total expenses, net profit or loss, profit margin %, income per acre and profit/loss per acre. Pre-loaded with a realistic rice crop example.

Income Categories

Crop sale: Main produce (grain, cotton, sugarcane, etc.). By-product sale: Straw, husk, cobs, bagasse. Government subsidy: PM-KISAN, state schemes, input subsidies. Agri-tourism: Farm visits, stays, experiences. Other income: Dairy, poultry, intercropping.

Expense Categories

Seeds: Seed cost per acre. Fertilizer: Urea, DAP, MOP, complexes. Pesticide: Insecticides, fungicides, herbicides. Irrigation: Electricity/diesel for pumps. Labour: Hired labour, family labour imputed. Harvesting: Combine harvester, manual harvesting. Transport: To mandi, storage. Land rent: If leased. Other: Interest, insurance, machinery maintenance.

Output Metrics

Total Income: Sum of all income sources. Total Expenses: Sum of all expense categories. Net Profit: Total Income - Total Expenses. Profit Margin %: (Net Profit ÷ Total Income) × 100. Income per Acre: Total Income ÷ Total Area. Profit per Acre: Net Profit ÷ Total Area.

Break-even Analysis

Shows the minimum yield or price needed to cover all costs. Helps farmers set target yields and negotiate minimum support prices.

Realistic Example

Pre-loaded example for 1 acre rice crop in Punjab: Yield 25 quintals, MSP ₹2,300/quintal = ₹57,500 income. Straw sale ₹5,000. Total income ₹62,500. Expenses: Seeds ₹2,500, Fertilizer ₹4,500, Pesticide ₹3,000, Irrigation ₹3,500, Labour ₹8,000, Harvesting ₹4,000, Transport ₹2,000. Total expenses ₹27,500. Net profit ₹35,000. Profit margin 56%.

faq.question: What expenses should I include?
faq.answer: Seeds, fertilizer, pesticide, irrigation (electricity/diesel), hired labour, harvesting (manual or machine), transport to mandi, and land rent if applicable.

faq.question: What income sources should I include?
faq.answer: Main crop sale, by-product sale (straw, husk, cobs), any government subsidy received, and income from intercropping or companion crops.

faq.question: What is profit margin in farming?
faq.answer: Profit margin = (Net Profit / Total Income) × 100. A healthy farm business typically targets 30–50% margin on input costs.

faq.question: How do I calculate profit per acre?
faq.answer: Divide total net profit by total cultivated area. For multi-crop farms, allocate costs proportionally to each crop.

faq.question: What is break-even yield?
faq.answer: The minimum yield (kg/acre) at which total income equals total expenses. Below this yield, the farm incurs a loss.

tools.name: Livestock Feed Calculator
tools.shortDescription: Calculate daily feed ration and monthly feed cost for cows, buffaloes, goats, poultry and other livestock.
tools.seoTitle: Free Livestock Feed Calculator India — Cow Buffalo Goat Feed Cost | UtilityMega
tools.metaDescription: Calculate daily green fodder, dry fodder, concentrate and mineral mixture requirements for cows, buffaloes, goats, sheep, pigs, poultry and bullocks. Monthly feed cost included.
tools.content:
Calculates daily feed ration (green fodder, dry fodder/straw, concentrate feed, mineral mixture) for 7 animal types: HF/Jersey cow, buffalo, goat, sheep, pig, poultry, and bullock. Adjusts ration based on body weight, production purpose (maintenance, milk production 5L or 10L/day, meat, work animal, pregnant) and season (summer +10%, winter +5%). Shows monthly feed cost per animal and total for the herd.

Animal Types

HF/Jersey cow: 400-600 kg, high milk production. Buffalo: 500-700 kg, higher fat milk. Goat: 20-50 kg, small ruminant. Sheep: 20-50 kg, wool and meat. Pig: 50-200 kg, meat production. Poultry: 1-3 kg, eggs or meat. Bullock: 400-600 kg, draft animal.

Feed Components

Green fodder: Fresh grass, maize, sorghum, legume fodder. Provides bulk, fibre, and some nutrients. Dry fodder/straw: Wheat straw, rice straw, hay. Provides fibre for rumen function. Concentrate feed: Grains (maize, wheat), oilcakes (soybean, groundnut), bran. Provides energy and protein. Mineral mixture: Calcium, phosphorus, trace minerals, vitamins. Essential for production and reproduction.

Production Purpose Adjustments

Maintenance: Basic feed for survival. Milk production (5L/day): Additional feed for 5 litres milk. Milk production (10L/day): Additional feed for 10 litres milk. Meat production: Higher energy for weight gain. Work animal: Extra energy for draft work. Pregnant: Additional nutrients for foetal development.

Seasonal Adjustments

Summer (+10%): Animals need more water and energy to maintain body temperature. Winter (+5%): Extra energy for thermoregulation. Monsoon: Standard ration, but ensure dry shelter and quality feed.

Monthly Feed Cost Calculation

Cost per kg × daily quantity × 30 days = monthly cost per animal. For herd, sum across all animals. Helps farmers budget for feed expenses.

Feeding Guidelines

Always provide clean, fresh water ad libitum. Introduce new feeds gradually over 7-10 days. Maintain consistent feeding times. Store feed in dry, rodent-proof containers. Check for mould or spoilage before feeding.

faq.question: How much feed does a dairy cow need per day?
faq.answer: A 450 kg HF/Jersey cow producing 5L milk/day needs approximately 25 kg green fodder, 7.5 kg dry straw and 5 kg concentrate feed daily.

faq.question: What is a mineral mixture and why is it needed?
faq.answer: Mineral mixture provides calcium, phosphorus, sodium, trace minerals and vitamins that are deficient in fodder. It prevents milk fever, reproductive failure and improves production.

faq.question: How does season affect feed requirement?
faq.answer: Animals need 10% more feed in summer to maintain body temperature and production. In winter, 5% extra energy is needed for thermoregulation.

faq.question: What is the difference between green and dry fodder?
faq.answer: Green fodder is fresh grass or cultivated fodder (60-80% moisture). Dry fodder is straw or hay (10-15% moisture). Both are needed for proper rumen function.

faq.question: How much concentrate feed for a buffalo?
faq.answer: A 600 kg buffalo producing 8-10L milk/day needs 8-10 kg concentrate feed daily, along with 30-40 kg green fodder and 8-10 kg dry fodder.

faq.question: What are Kharif and Rabi crops?

faq.question: What is MSP (Minimum Support Price)?
faq.answer: MSP is the government-guaranteed minimum price at which the government (through FCI, NAFED etc.) procures crops from farmers. It protects farmers from distress selling when market prices fall.

faq.question: Are MSP rates guaranteed to every farmer?
faq.answer: MSP is a price guarantee but actual procurement depends on state government agencies and FCI centres. Farmers in states with active procurement (Punjab, Haryana, MP) are more likely to sell at MSP.

faq.question: How often are MSP rates revised?

faq.question: What is A2+FL cost?

tools.content:
Encode and decode Base64 for text strings, file uploads (with data URI output), and image preview. Supports standard and URL-safe Base64 variants (replaces + with - and / with _). Three modes: text encode/decode, file encode, and image preview.

Base64 Encoding

Converts binary data to ASCII text using 64 characters (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, +, /). Used for embedding binary data in text-based formats like JSON, XML, HTML, and email.

URL-Safe Base64

Replaces + with - and / with _ so the output can be safely used in URLs and filenames without percent-encoding. No padding (=) issues. Commonly used in JWTs, OAuth tokens, and API keys.

File Encoding

Upload any file (image, PDF, document) and get its Base64 representation. Output includes data URI format (data:image/png;base64,...) for direct use in HTML/CSS.

Image Preview

When decoding Base64 that represents an image, the tool automatically shows a preview. Useful for verifying image data before using in applications.

Common Use Cases

Email attachments: MIME encoding. API authentication: Basic Auth headers (username:password encoded). Data URLs: Embed images in HTML/CSS. JWT tokens: URL-safe Base64 for header and payload. Database storage: Store binary data as text.

faq.question: Is Base64 the same as encryption?
faq.answer: No. Base64 is encoding — it converts binary to text using a reversible algorithm. Anyone can decode it. Never use Base64 for sensitive data without actual encryption.

faq.question: Why does Base64 end with == signs?
faq.answer: Base64 works in 3-byte groups. Padding (=) is added when input length is not a multiple of 3. URL-safe Base64 often omits padding.

faq.question: What is URL-safe Base64?
faq.answer: URL-safe Base64 replaces + with - and / with _ so the output can be safely used in URLs without percent-encoding. Used in JWTs and OAuth tokens.

faq.question: What is a data URI?
faq.answer: A data URI is a Base64-encoded resource embedded directly in HTML/CSS. Format: data:[mediatype];base64,[data]. Used for small images and fonts.

faq.question: Can I decode files larger than 10MB?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool handles large files in the browser. For very large files (100MB+), processing time may increase based on your device's performance.

tools.content:
Generate cryptographically secure UUIDs in v4 (random), v1 (time-based), v5 (name-based), or ULID (sortable) formats. Bulk generate up to 100 at once. Format options include uppercase, removing dashes, and adding braces. Includes a UUID validator.

UUID Versions

UUID v4 (Random): Most common — 122 bits of random data. Cryptographically secure. Ideal for database keys, session IDs, and general unique identifiers.

UUID v1 (Time-based): Based on timestamp and MAC address. Sortable by creation time. May reveal system information. Use when order matters.

UUID v5 (Name-based): SHA-1 hash of namespace + name. Deterministic — same input produces same UUID. Use for generating consistent IDs from namespaces.

ULID (Universally Unique Lexicographically Sortable Identifier): 26-character string with timestamp (48 bits) and randomness (80 bits). Sortable by creation time. URL-safe, no hyphens.

Format Options

Uppercase: All letters in uppercase (e.g., 123E4567-E89B-12D3-A456-426614174000). No dashes: Remove hyphens for compact representation (e.g., 123e4567e89b12d3a456426614174000). Braces: Wrap in curly braces { } for some systems.

Bulk Generation

Generate up to 100 UUIDs at once. Copy all to clipboard or download as JSON/CSV. Useful for test data generation and database seeding.

UUID Validator

Check if a string is a valid UUID. Identifies which version (v1-v5) the UUID belongs to. Validates format and checksum where applicable.

Use Cases

Database primary keys: UUID v4 for distributed systems. API request IDs: ULID for sortable tracing. Consistent IDs: UUID v5 for deterministic generation. Test data: Bulk generation for unit tests.

faq.question: What is the difference between UUID v1 and v4?
faq.answer: UUID v1 includes a timestamp and MAC address — sortable but potentially reveals system info. UUID v4 is fully random — most common for security-sensitive IDs.

faq.question: Are generated UUIDs truly unique?
faq.answer: UUID v4 has 2^122 possible values. The probability of collision is astronomically small — practically guaranteed unique for all real-world use cases.

faq.question: What is a ULID?
faq.answer: ULID (Universally Unique Lexicographically Sortable Identifier) is a 26-character alternative to UUID that is sortable by creation time — ideal for database primary keys.

faq.question: Can I generate UUIDs without hyphens?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the 'No dashes' format option to generate UUIDs without hyphens (32 characters).

faq.question: What is the maximum number of UUIDs I can generate at once?
faq.answer: Bulk generation supports up to 100 UUIDs at a time. For larger volumes, generate in batches.

tools.content:
Build cron expressions with a visual 5-field editor. Get a plain-English description of your schedule and preview the next 8 run times. Includes 16 common schedule presets and a full syntax reference.

Cron Fields

Minute (0-59): When to run the job. Hour (0-23): Hour of the day. Day of Month (1-31): Day of the month. Month (1-12 or JAN-DEC): Month of the year. Day of Week (0-7 or SUN-SAT): Day of the week (0 and 7 both represent Sunday).

Special Characters

* (asterisk): Any value. , (comma): Value list separator. - (hyphen): Range of values. / (slash): Step values. Example: */15 in minute field = every 15 minutes.

Preset Schedules

Every minute: * * * * *. Every hour: 0 * * * *. Every day at midnight: 0 0 * * *. Every Sunday at midnight: 0 0 * * 0. Every Monday at 9am: 0 9 * * 1. Every weekday at 9am: 0 9 * * 1-5. First day of month at 12am: 0 0 1 * *. Last day of month at 12am: 0 0 L * *.

Human-Readable Description

Converts cron expressions to plain English: "At 09:00 on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday." "Every 15 minutes between 09:00 and 17:00." "At 00:00 on the first day of every month."

Next Run Times

Shows the next 8 scheduled run times based on the current time. Useful for verifying the schedule behaves as expected.

Linux vs AWS EventBridge

Linux cron uses 5 fields (minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week). AWS EventBridge uses 6 fields (adds year) and uses ? for unspecified day fields. The tool supports both formats.

faq.question: What is a cron expression?
faq.answer: A cron expression is a string of 5 fields defining when a scheduled task runs: minute, hour, day-of-month, month, and day-of-week.

faq.question: How do I run a job every 5 minutes?
faq.answer: Use */5 in the minute field: `*/5 * * * *`. The */ syntax means 'every N' of that unit.

faq.question: What is the difference between Linux cron and AWS EventBridge?
faq.answer: AWS EventBridge uses a 6-field format (adds year) and uses ? for unspecified day fields. Standard Linux cron uses 5 fields.

faq.question: How do I run a job on the first day of every month?
faq.answer: Use `0 0 1 * *` — at 00:00 on day 1 of every month.

faq.question: How do I run a job on weekdays only?
faq.answer: Use `0 9 * * 1-5` — at 09:00 on Monday through Friday.

Color Formats

faq.question: What is the difference between RGB and CMYK?
faq.answer: RGB is an additive model for screens (light). CMYK is subtractive for print (ink). The same color can look different in each medium.

tools.content:
Decode any JWT token to inspect its header, payload claims, and signature. Automatically detects algorithm, parses timestamp claims (exp, iat, nbf) into human-readable dates, and shows expiry status. Color-coded visualization of all three JWT parts.

JWT Structure

Header: Contains algorithm (alg) and token type (typ). Common algorithms: HS256, HS384, HS512, RS256, RS384, RS512, ES256, ES384, ES512.

Payload: Contains claims. Registered claims: iss (issuer), sub (subject), aud (audience), exp (expiration time), nbf (not before time), iat (issued at time), jti (JWT ID). Public claims: Custom claims defined by applications.

Signature: Verifies token authenticity. The decoder shows the signature but does not verify it (requires secret/public key).

Timestamp Parsing

Expiration (exp): Shows expiry date/time and indicates if token is expired. Issued At (iat): Shows when token was created. Not Before (nbf): Shows when token becomes valid.

Algorithm Detection

Automatically detects algorithm from header. Shows algorithm type (HMAC, RSA, ECDSA). Validates that algorithm is properly formatted.

Security Notes

Decoding only reveals the token contents. Signature verification requires the secret key (for HS256) or public key (for RS256/ES256). Never share your JWT secret key. Never paste production tokens with sensitive data into any online tool without understanding the risks.

faq.question: Can I verify a JWT signature?
faq.answer: Decoding shows the header and payload without signature verification. Signature verification requires the secret key (HMAC) or public key (RSA/ECDSA).

faq.question: Is it safe to paste my JWT here?
faq.answer: All decoding happens in your browser — nothing is sent to servers. Still, avoid pasting production tokens with sensitive data into any online tool.

faq.question: What claims are in a JWT?
faq.answer: Standard claims: sub (subject), iss (issuer), exp (expiry), iat (issued at), aud (audience). Applications add custom claims for user roles, permissions, etc.

faq.question: What does the expiration claim show?
faq.answer: The exp claim shows when the token expires. The tool displays the expiration date/time and indicates if the token is expired or still valid.

faq.question: What algorithms are supported?
faq.answer: All JWT algorithms: HS256, HS384, HS512, RS256, RS384, RS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, and none (unsecured).

Password Strength Metrics

Entropy (bits): Measures randomness. Each additional bit doubles the possible combinations. 60+ bits is strong for online accounts. 80+ bits for sensitive accounts. 100+ bits for encryption keys.

Crack Time Estimates

Online attack: 1,000 guesses/second (typical website rate). Offline fast hash: 10,000,000,000 guesses/second (dedicated GPU cracker). Shows time for both scenarios to illustrate importance of password strength.

Character Sets

Uppercase: A-Z (26 chars). Lowercase: a-z (26 chars). Numbers: 0-9 (10 chars). Symbols: !@#$%^&*()_+~`-=[]{};:'",./<>? (32 chars). More character types = higher entropy.

Ambiguous Characters

Exclude confusing characters: i, I, l, L, 1, o, O, 0, etc. Helps avoid confusion when copying passwords manually.

Passphrase Mode

Generate memorable 4-word passphrases from a dictionary of 7,776 common words. Example: "correct-horse-battery-staple". Provides ~52 bits of entropy (4 words × 13 bits per word). Easier to remember than random strings.

Batch Generation

faq.question: What makes a password secure?
faq.answer: Length is the most important factor. A 20-character random string is far stronger than an 8-character complex one. Use 16+ characters with mixed charsets for high-security accounts.

faq.question: What is a passphrase?
faq.answer: A passphrase combines 4+ random words (e.g., 'correct-horse-battery-staple'). It provides ~52 bits of entropy and is easier to remember than a random string.

faq.question: What is password entropy?
faq.answer: Entropy measures unpredictability in bits. Each additional bit doubles the possible combinations. 60+ bits is strong for online accounts; 100+ bits for encryption keys.

faq.question: Should I use symbols in passwords?
faq.answer: Yes, if the system allows them. Adding symbols increases entropy. However, length matters more than complexity. A 20-character lowercase password is stronger than a 10-character password with all character types.

Improvement Suggestions

Increase length to at least 12-16 characters. Add numbers and symbols. Avoid dictionary words. Avoid personal information (name, birthdate). Avoid common patterns. Use passphrase approach for memorable strong passwords.

Generate HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) using SHA-256, SHA-512, or SHA-384. Output in Hex, Base64, or URL-safe Base64URL format. Built-in HMAC verification tool. Auto-generates a secure random key. Explains how HMAC works and shows common use cases: webhook verification (GitHub, Stripe), AWS API signing, JWT HS256/HS512.

How HMAC Works

HMAC = hash(secret + message). It ensures both authenticity (only someone with the secret key could have created it) and integrity (message hasn't been tampered with). Unlike a regular hash, HMAC requires a shared secret key.

Algorithms Supported

SHA-256: 256-bit output, standard for most APIs (GitHub, Stripe, AWS). SHA-512: 512-bit output, stronger security margin. SHA-384: 384-bit output, truncated SHA-512.

Output Formats

Hex: Hexadecimal string (64 chars for SHA-256). Base64: Standard Base64 encoding (44 chars for SHA-256). Base64URL: URL-safe Base64 (replaces + with -, / with _, no padding).

Use Cases

Webhook verification: GitHub, Stripe, and other services sign webhook payloads with HMAC to verify authenticity. API authentication: AWS Signature V4 uses HMAC-SHA256. JWT: HS256 and HS512 algorithms use HMAC. Data integrity: Verify files or messages haven't been tampered with.

HMAC Verification

Paste your HMAC output and the tool will verify if it matches the expected signature for your message and secret key. Helps debug API signing issues.

faq.question: What is HMAC used for?
faq.answer: HMAC proves that a message was created by someone with the secret key and wasn't tampered with in transit. Used for API authentication and webhook payload verification.

faq.question: How is HMAC different from a regular hash?
faq.answer: A regular hash has no secret key — anyone can compute it. HMAC requires a shared secret, providing authentication that a hash alone cannot.

faq.question: HMAC-SHA256 vs HMAC-SHA512?
faq.answer: HMAC-SHA256 is the standard for most APIs (AWS, GitHub, Stripe). Use HMAC-SHA512 for additional security margin when performance allows.

faq.question: What is Base64URL encoding?
faq.answer: Base64URL replaces + with -, / with _, and removes padding. It's safe to use in URLs, cookies, and JSON without escaping.

faq.question: Is the secret key stored anywhere?
faq.answer: No. All processing happens in your browser. The secret key is never transmitted to any server.

faq.question: When should I renew my SSL certificate?
faq.answer: Renew at least 30 days before expiry. Let's Encrypt auto-renews at 30 days. Paid certificates should be renewed 60+ days before expiry to allow processing time.

faq.question: What causes SSL certificate errors?
faq.answer: Expired certificate, domain mismatch, self-signed certificate (not trusted by browsers), broken certificate chain, or a revoked certificate.

faq.question: What is TLS 1.3?
faq.answer: TLS 1.3 (released 2018) is the latest version. It's faster (1-RTT handshake) and more secure than TLS 1.2. All modern browsers support it.

faq.question: What are Subject Alternative Names (SANs)?
faq.answer: SANs list all domain names a certificate is valid for. A single certificate can cover multiple domains (e.g., example.com, www.example.com, api.example.com).

faq.question: What is HSTS?
faq.answer: HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) forces browsers to always use HTTPS connections, preventing SSL stripping attacks and protocol downgrade attacks.

CORS Headers Checked

Access-Control-Allow-Origin: Which origins are allowed (must match your origin or *). Access-Control-Allow-Methods: Which HTTP methods are allowed (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.). Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Which request headers are allowed. Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: Whether cookies/auth headers are allowed. Access-Control-Max-Age: How long preflight results are cached. Access-Control-Expose-Headers: Which response headers are exposed to JavaScript.

Preflight OPTIONS Request

Checks if the server properly responds to OPTIONS requests. Required for cross-origin requests with non-simple methods (POST with custom headers, PUT, DELETE).

Server Configuration Fixes

Express.js/Node.js: `app.use(cors())` or custom middleware. Nginx: `add_header Access-Control-Allow-Origin *;` Apache: `Header set Access-Control-Allow-Origin "*"` Python/Flask: `@app.after_request` decorator. PHP: `header('Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *');`

Common CORS Errors

No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header: Server not configured for CORS. Origin not allowed: Server only allows specific origins, not yours. Credentials not allowed: Server doesn't allow cookies/auth headers. Preflight fails: OPTIONS request not handled properly.

Testing Notes

CORS tests are performed from your browser. Some APIs may block requests from browsers but work from servers. The tool shows exact request/response headers for debugging.

faq.question: What causes CORS errors?
faq.answer: The server is not returning the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header matching your origin, or the preflight OPTIONS request is not handled correctly.

faq.question: Can CORS be disabled in the browser?
faq.answer: Some extensions can disable CORS for testing. Never disable CORS in production — it is a critical security mechanism protecting users.

faq.question: Why does CORS only affect browsers?
faq.answer: CORS is enforced by browsers to protect users. Server-to-server requests and tools like curl are not subject to CORS restrictions.

faq.question: What is a preflight request?
faq.answer: A preflight OPTIONS request is sent before the actual request for non-simple requests (PUT, DELETE, custom headers). It checks if the server allows the actual request.

faq.question: What is the difference between * and a specific origin?
faq.answer: * allows any origin (broad). A specific origin (like https://example.com) allows only that origin. * cannot be used with credentials (cookies, auth headers).

tools.h1: CSP Generator — Content Security Policy Builder
tools.content:
Build a Content Security Policy with a visual checkbox interface covering all major directives: default-src, script-src, style-src, img-src, font-src, connect-src, and more. Shows security score (0–100), warns about unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval, outputs ready-to-use header string, HTML meta tag, and Nginx config. Includes 5 presets for SPA, WordPress, Google Fonts, minimal, and development modes.

CSP Directives

default-src: Fallback for other directives. script-src: Where JavaScript can load from. style-src: Where CSS can load from. img-src: Where images can load from. font-src: Where fonts can load from. connect-src: Where fetch/XHR requests can go. frame-src: Where iframes can load from. object-src: Where plugins can load from. base-uri: Where <base> tag can point to. form-action: Where forms can submit to. report-uri: Where violations are reported.

Source Values

'self': Same origin only. 'none': Nothing allowed. 'unsafe-inline': Inline scripts/styles (weak). 'unsafe-eval': eval() allowed (weak). 'strict-dynamic': Trust nonces/hashes. 'nonce-{random}': Specific random token. 'sha256-{hash}': Specific inline script hash. https://: Any HTTPS URL. https://example.com: Specific domain.

Security Score

0-100 rating based on policy strength. Penalties for: 'unsafe-inline' (-20), 'unsafe-eval' (-15), no 'strict-dynamic' (-10), wildcard '*' allowed (-25). Bonuses for: 'nonce-' or 'sha256-' (+15), 'report-uri' configured (+5).

Presets

SPA (Single Page App): Modern React/Vue configuration. WordPress: WordPress-specific with admin API allowances. Google Fonts: Includes fonts.googleapis.com and fonts.gstatic.com. Minimal: Very restrictive, least privilege. Development: Development-friendly with unsafe-eval and localhost.

Report-Only Mode

Start with Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only header to monitor violations without blocking. Allows testing policies in production safely. Violations are sent to report-uri endpoint.

faq.question: What does unsafe-inline mean in CSP?
faq.answer: It allows inline scripts and styles — significantly weakening XSS protection. Avoid unsafe-inline and use nonces or hashes instead when possible.

faq.question: How do I deploy CSP?
faq.answer: Add as an HTTP response header: Content-Security-Policy: your-policy. Or use a meta tag (with limitations). Start with report-only mode to monitor before enforcing.

faq.question: What is report-only mode?
faq.answer: Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only logs violations without blocking them. Use it to test your policy and find false positives before switching to enforcement.

faq.question: What is a nonce?
faq.answer: A nonce is a random, single-use token. CSP allows scripts with matching nonce='value' attribute. More secure than unsafe-inline.

faq.question: What is strict-dynamic?
faq.answer: strict-dynamic tells the browser to trust scripts loaded by scripts that already have a valid nonce or hash. Makes managing third-party scripts easier.

tools.name: Bcrypt Generator
tools.shortDescription: Generate bcrypt password hashes with configurable cost factor and verify passwords against bcrypt hashes.
tools.seoTitle: Free Bcrypt Generator — Hash & Verify Passwords Online | UtilityMega
tools.metaDescription: Generate bcrypt password hashes with cost factor 4–15. Verify passwords against bcrypt hashes. Shows hash anatomy, cost factor timing guide, and explains why bcrypt is preferred over SHA.
tools.content:
Generate bcrypt hashes with configurable cost factor (4–15). Shows estimated computation time per cost level. Visual hash anatomy breaking down the version, cost, salt, and hash portions. Cost factor guide shows recommended settings. Verify passwords against existing bcrypt hashes. Explains why bcrypt is superior to SHA for password storage.

Why Bcrypt?

Bcrypt is designed for password hashing. It's intentionally slow and includes a salt to prevent rainbow table attacks. Unlike SHA (which is fast, allowing billions of guesses/second), bcrypt's work factor can be increased as hardware improves to maintain security.

Hash Anatomy

$2a$10$N9qo8uLOickgx2ZMRZoMy.Mr/F9p8q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q

$2a$: Version (2a, 2b, 2y). $10$: Cost factor (2^10 = 1,024 rounds). N9qo8uLOickgx2ZMRZoMy.: Salt (22 characters). Mr/F9p8q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q4q: Hash (31 characters).

Cost Factor Guide

Cost 4-7: Too weak — not recommended. Cost 8: ~40ms, minimum for development. Cost 10: ~100ms, recommended for standard web apps. Cost 12: ~400ms, for high-security systems. Cost 14-15: ~1-3 seconds, for maximum security.

Verification

Enter a password and a bcrypt hash to verify if they match. The tool extracts the salt from the hash and recomputes the hash for comparison. Useful for debugging stored passwords and testing implementations.

Security Best Practices

Always use bcrypt for password storage (or Argon2id). Never use MD5 or SHA1 for passwords. Use cost factor that keeps hash time under 100ms on your servers. Increase cost factor over time as hardware improves.

faq.question: Why use bcrypt instead of SHA for passwords?
faq.answer: SHA is fast — attackers can compute billions per second. Bcrypt is intentionally slow, limiting attackers to thousands/second. This makes brute-force attacks practically infeasible.

faq.question: What cost factor should I use?
faq.answer: Cost 10 (~100ms) for standard web apps. Cost 12 (~400ms) for high-security systems. Increase cost factor as hardware improves to maintain ~100ms target.

faq.question: Are generated hashes unique for the same password?
faq.answer: Yes. Bcrypt automatically generates a unique random salt for each hash, so hashing the same password twice produces different hashes — preventing rainbow table attacks.

faq.question: What is the difference between bcrypt versions?
faq.answer: $2a$: Original spec. $2b$: Fixed bug for Unicode passwords. $2y$: Used by some PHP implementations. All are compatible with modern libraries.

faq.question: Can I migrate from SHA to bcrypt?
faq.answer: Yes. Hash existing passwords with bcrypt when users log in next time. Store both until all passwords are migrated. This provides a seamless upgrade path.

faq.answer: Encode text before inserting into HTML to prevent XSS attacks: < becomes <, > becomes >, & becomes &. Always encode user input in HTML output.

faq.question: What is the difference between URL encode and HTML encode?
faq.answer: URL encoding converts characters for safe use in URLs (%20 for space). HTML encoding converts characters for safe display in HTML (< for <). They're used in different contexts.

faq.question: What is ROT13?
faq.answer: ROT13 is a simple Caesar cipher that shifts letters 13 positions. Since 13+13=26 (full alphabet), encoding and decoding use the same operation. It's used for spoiler text and simple obfuscation.

faq.question: What do the colors mean?
faq.answer: Green background: lines added in the modified version. Red background: lines removed (present in original, not in modified). No color: unchanged lines present in both.

faq.question: What is the difference between line, word and character diff?
faq.answer: Line diff compares whole lines — best for code and documents. Word diff shows which words changed within lines. Character diff shows individual character changes — most granular, best for short texts.

faq.question: Is this tool good for comparing code?
faq.answer: Yes for simple comparisons. For advanced code diffing with syntax highlighting, indentation-aware comparison and git-style output, use the Developer Tools diff checker which has more programming-specific features.
